question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any interim measures being considered so that children attending the Irish Society's Primary School, Coleraine, can access full-time nursery provision in advance of the new building being completed.
answer_text: The Irish Society PS NU is one of 102 settings I have approved to transition to full-time hours (22.5 hrs per week) from September 2026, or as soon as possible thereafter. The Irish Society PS NU will require capital investment for an additional classroom to facilitate the transition to the full-time session length. Due to the nature of capital investment and building works, it is likely that the Irish Society PS NU may take longer to complete the transition than some other settings; this was acknowledged in the school’s request to transition and in my approval of it. The Education Authority will engage with the school to make the necessary changes to complete transition as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any reason for the delay in implementing full-time nursery provision at the Irish Society's Primary School, Coleraine, despite approval being granted in December 2025.
answer_text: The Irish Society PS NU is one of 102 settings I have approved to transition to full-time hours (22.5 hrs per week) from September 2026, or as soon as possible thereafter. The Irish Society PS NU will require capital investment for an additional classroom to facilitate the transition to the full-time session length. Due to the nature of capital investment and building works, it is likely that the Irish Society PS NU may take longer to complete the transition than some other settings; this was acknowledged in the school’s request to transition and in my approval of it. The Education Authority will engage with the school to make the necessary changes to complete transition as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the waiting time for a Counter Terrorist Check security clearance to be issued in Northern Ireland, in the last three years; and (ii) any steps her Department is taking to reduce delays.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information relating to the waiting time for a Counter Terrorist Check (CTC) security clearance to be issued in Northern Ireland as CTC applications are processed by a number of different public sector bodies for their respective organisations.However, I can confirm that the average waiting times for CTC security clearances processed by my Department in the last three business years are:2023-24 – 14 weeks;2024-25 – 20 weeks; and2025-26 – 13 weeks.My officials take the following steps in an effort to reduce delays:Direct liaison with individuals, where they fail to engage promptly with the application process, to ask them to complete and submit their applications without further delay;Direct liaison with the vetting provider, i.e. UK Security Vetting, and individuals to ensure that they respond promptly to requests for additional information, so as not to prolong application processes.It is my understanding that HR Connect processes CTC security clearance applications for NICS Human Resources appointments to the wider NICS.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will consider measures similar to the temporary ten per cent uplift provided to home to school transport operators during the COVID 19 period, in order to support service sustainability in light of current cost pressures.
answer_text: The responsibility for the operation and delivery of home to school transport rests with the Education Authority (EA), including the management of contracts it holds with private transport operators. The EA has advised me that it recognises concerns raised by private transport operators in relation to recent fuel price increases and it is monitoring developments in fuel costs to assess any implications for existing arrangements. At present, however, the EA has advised that it has no plans to introduce a temporary increase in payments to private operators.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether any assessment has been made of the impact of recent fuel price increases, arising from instability in the Middle East, on private coach operators providing home to school transport.
answer_text: The responsibility for the operation and delivery of home to school transport rests with the Education Authority (EA), including the management of contracts it holds with private transport operators. The EA has advised me that it recognises concerns raised by private transport operators in relation to recent fuel price increases and it is monitoring developments in fuel costs to assess any implications for existing arrangements. At present, however, the EA has advised that it has no plans to introduce a temporary increase in payments to private operators.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline any evidence his Department has that shows the Phone First service has reduced attendance at emergency departments.
answer_text: I recognise the vital work that community pharmacy teams do each day to ensure the safe supply of medicines to patients. This is reflected in the considerable investment made by my Department over recent years to strengthen the role of the sector in primary care, including yearly increases to recurrent funding, annual pay uplifts aligned with DDRB, and exceptional investments such as for National Insurance increases. Government funding for community pharmacy HSC service provision has increased by almost £60m (57%) since April 2018. This is the highest level of funding to date and is commensurate with the rest of the UK.Community pharmacies access medicines via a range of commercial arrangements with different pharmaceutical wholesalers operating within a UK-wide supply chain for medicines.These commercial arrangements are agreed between the wholesaler and community pharmacy business and operate in a competitive market. Wholesalers compete on price, service levels, delivery speed and credit terms.Department officials engage with wholesalers to ensure that the continuity of supply of medicines to patients and members of the public is not affected, notable examples include work related to EU Exit, the Covid Pandemic and at times of medicines shortages. Wholesalers have advised of pressures within the community pharmacy sector across the UK, including NI. Claims that 90% of pharmacy customers at one wholesaler did not pay their bill in January are concerning for the company involved. My officials will continue to engage with wholesalers to ensure that the continuity of supply of medicines to patients and members of the public is assured.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the reason why newly-qualified nurses are reportedly facing challenges in securing first posts despite ongoing workforce shortages.
answer_text: Individual HSC Trusts, as the employer, are responsible for recruitment across all healthcare professions, including newly qualified nurses, within HSCNI. It is worth acknowledging the significant progress which has been made in recent years to stabilise and grow the HSC workforce. The Department’s strategic approach to workforce development, published in 2018, supported by sustained increased investment in pre-registration education and training, has facilitated a 19.5% increase in whole time equivalent staff directly employed across the HSC. That amounts to 11,063 additional whole time equivalent staff, including 1,342 (+32.0%) more medical and dental staff, 3,257 (+21.6%) more registered nurses and midwives, 2,213 (+27.2%) more professional and technical staff including allied health professionals and 969 (+25.7%) more social workers, directly employed by the HSC.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of Emergency Nurse Practitioner posts that there are in Emergency Departments; and (ii) the number of these that are vacant.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale as to why the Causeway Hospital has had no Emergency Nurse Practitioner recruitment competitions for over a year.

To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department has had with SONI, regarding electricity transmission constraints affecting economic development in the North Coast, in the last three years.

To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department has carried out any assessment on whether electricity network constraints are affecting the ability to attract inward investment to the Causeway Coast and Glens local council area.
answer_text: My department recognises that electricity network capacity and resilience are important enablers of economic development and inward investment, including for industrial and manufacturing activity. My department therefore engages closely with the system operator, SONI, NIE Networks and the Utility Regulator on system-wide network constraints and grid development, including those affecting the North Coast and North-West.These issues are reflected in SONI’s Transmission Development Plan 2025–2034, which identifies the need for strategic transmission reinforcement in the North Coast and North-West, including projects such as the Coleraine and Limavady transformers, the Coolkeeragh 110kV extension and wider North-West reinforcements.To support delivery of this critical infrastructure, I established the Grid Development Monitoring Group, with senior representation from SONI, NIE Networks and the Utility Regulator, to improve coordination and help accelerate delivery of essential network upgrades needed to support decarbonisation and economic growth.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the current electricity grid capacity, available for new industrial and manufacturing developments, in the Causeway Coast and Glens local council area.
answer_text: Connection capacity for new industrial and manufacturing developments is not held by my department at local council area level and is assessed on a case-by-case basis by SONI and NIE Networks.My department recognises that parts of the Causeway Coast and Glens area are affected by electricity network constraints, particularly in the North Coast and North-West. These constraints are reflected in SONI’s Transmission Development Plan 2025–2034, which sets out a programme of strategic transmission investment in these areas, including projects such as the Coleraine and Limavady transformers, the Coolkeeragh 110kV extension and wider North-West reinforcements.My department is working closely with SONI, NIE Networks and the Utility Regulator to support the delivery of these projects, including through the Grid Development Monitoring Group, which provides oversight of progress on critical grid infrastructure needed to support economic development and decarbonisation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) how his Department monitors the impact of private urgent care centres on patients from lower income households; and (ii) whether any safeguards are in place to prevent a two-tier model of urgent care.
To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has evaluated whether private urgent care centres reduce pressures on Emergency Departments, or simply redirect patient flow.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of requests for review of homelessness decisions currently outstanding within the Housing Executive, for applicants in East Londonderry; (ii) the (a) average; and (b) longest, waiting times are for the completion of reviews, in the last 12 months; and (iii) any measures his Department is taking to reduce any backlog.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“(i) There are 10 requests for review of homelessness decisions outstanding in East Londonderry as of 12 March 2026, 6 from 2025 and 4 from 2026.(ii)(a) 114 days. (ii)(b) 192 days.(iii) Homelessness Review Officers are concentrating in dealing with all outstanding cases once all necessary background information has been obtained.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 40800/22-27, given that NI Water has identified the necessary pumping station and sewer upgrades for Christie Park, Coleraine, to detail the reason why no clear timetable has yet been provided for delivery.
answer_text: The management of the public water and wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. I have sought further information from NI Water to inform the response to your question.NI Water has advised that the upgrades to the Strand Road and Waterside Wastewater Pumping Stations were originally included within the current price control period (PC21) but have been unable to proceed due to budgetary constraints. This project is being prioritised for the next price control period (PC28) subject to a successful outcome for the price control and budget availability. As outlined in my previous response on this topic (AQW 40799/22-27 refers), in the interim NI Water is progressing the detailed project design phase and engagement with NIEA on the proposed solution.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 40800/22-27, whether her Department or NI Water has identified any additional short-term measures that could protect the River Bann and the Christie Park community, until the full upgrade works are delivered.
answer_text: The management of the public water and wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. I have sought further information from NI Water to inform the response to your question.As advised in AQW 40800/22-27, NI Water has undertaken desilting on the public sewer line in the Christie Park area. A major desilting was completed in January 2026. NI Water has also installed event duration monitors (EDMs) on the Strand Road Wastewater Pumping Station (WwPS) and Strand Road Two Coleraine Combined Sewer Overflow (CSO) to monitor the frequency and duration of spills. Further cleaning is planned for June and will be scheduled sooner if deemed necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on any steps her Department is taking to streamline the sharing of police reports with housing providers, in light of delays hindering effective action against anti-social behaviour.

To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered rescinding Penalty Charge Notices for patients who have received tickets at Causeway Hospital, on days when there is not enough parking to accommodate clinics running.
answer_text: The aim of the Department of Health’s policy on car parking is to ensure the effective provision of car parking across all of the Health Estate for patients, visitors and staff. Decisions on how the Department’s policy is applied is for each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust to determine to enable them to take local issues into account.Parking enforcement on hospital sites, including Causeway Hospital, is focused on areas where staff, visitors, and patients have parked inappropriately, such as on yellow lines, clearly marked drop off areas, or in disabled bays designated for Blue Badge holders. The purpose of this enforcement is to maintain safe and unobstructed access routes for blue-light vehicles, ambulances, and essential contractors, supporting the Trust’s ability to deliver critical services without disruption.Patients who believe they have received a Parking Charge Notice (PCN) unfairly can submit an appeal, as outlined on the PCN and enforcement signage across the Causeway Hospital site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any assessment he has made of the impact of ongoing parking pressures at Causeway Hospital on patient safety and timely access to care.
answer_text: The aim of the Department of Health’s policy on car parking is to ensure the effective provision of car parking across all of the Health Estate for patients, visitors and staff. Decisions on how the Department’s policy is applied is for each Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust to determine to enable them to take local issues into account.To manage the impact of parking pressures at Causeway Hospital, the Northern HSC Trust have secured capital funding for the development of an additional 90 –100 space car park to the rear of the site, with construction expected to commence in Spring 2026, subject to securing the required planning approval.Car park staff are deployed on site during peak periods to assist patients and visitors in accessing available parking spaces and to help manage traffic flow safely and efficiently.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether additional signage has been considered at Causeway Hospital, to advise of the level of parking availability; and (ii) whether consideration has been given to advising patients, on appointment letters to allow time for parking.
answer_text: The Northern Health & Social Care Trust has confirmed that additional signage directing patients and visitors to the carparking area at the rear of the site, where there is generally less pressure on spaces, is currently under consideration. In addition, appointment letters, issued by the Booking Office, advise patients that hospital car parking can be very busy during clinic and visiting times, and that they should allow sufficient time for parking to ensure they arrive for their appointment on time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the rationale as to why care workers in reablement teams in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust are experiencing reduced rotas, while care packages are increasingly outsourced to private providers; and (ii) any urgent measures he intends take to address the situation whereby patients remain in hospital due to the unavailability of care packages despite claims that there is sufficient care provision within the health service.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on NI Water's wastewater pumping station and sewer upgrades needed to resolve ongoing issues at Christie Park, Coleraine.
answer_text: The management of the public water and wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. I have sought further information from NI Water to inform the response to your question.NI Water advised it has identified the wastewater pumping station and sewer upgrades required to address the issues at Christie Park. The detailed project design phase is ongoing, and agreement will be needed with NIEA on the proposed solution. NI Water is prioritising these upgrades as part of the upcoming Price Control Period (PC28) and is engaging with key stakeholders as part of the planning for this period. As a mitigation exercise in the interim, NI Water undertakes desilting on the public sewer line in the Christie Park area. A major desilting was completed in January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has made of the environmental and public health risks around Christie Park, Coleraine, if the wastewater infrastructure upgrades continue to be delayed.
answer_text: The management of the public water and wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. I have sought further information from NI Water to inform the response to your question.NI Water has advised that it has recently completed a Drainage Area Plan (DAP) for the Coleraine catchment to help better understand the environmental and hydraulic risks in the area. As part of this work, the performance of the discharging assets, including Strand Road Wastewater Pumping Station (WwPS) and Strand Road Two Coleraine Combined Sewer Overflow (CSO), was assessed. Findings were shared with the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) for their consideration of any required environmental improvements.The spills to the River Bann were not identified by NIEA as requiring reduction. NIEA has, however, requested NI Water install additional storage to provide resilience in the event of pump failure, and an extension to the outfall pipe. Planned upgrades to the wastewater pumping station and sewer system are being prioritised by NI Water for the next Price Control period (PC28).In the interim, NI Water has undertaken desilting on the public sewer line in the Christie Park area. A major desilting was completed in January 2026. NI Water has also installed event duration monitors (EDMs) on the Strand Road WwPS and Strand Road Two Coleraine CSO to monitor the frequency and duration of spills. These monitors will assist in assessing any risks to the local environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on when she will announce the projects that will be supported through the Enhanced Investment Zone.
answer_text: No projects have been approved at this stage, as it is important to secure full programme approval before moving to individual project approvals. However, a number of high-potential projects have been identified, on which we are continuing to engage with private-sector partners. Due to commercial sensitivities, I cannot provide details of those partners.Once the necessary business cases are completed and approved, I will be in a position to announce individual projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the proposed eligibility criteria for the Warm Healthy Homes Fund.
answer_text: My officials are currently developing the detailed design of the Warm Healthy Homes Fund: an energy efficiency scheme to improve warmth, health outcomes and energy affordability for low-income households. This work, including the proposed eligibility criteria, will be further informed and refined through a public consultation to be undertaken in the next few months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps he will take to ensure the Warm Healthy Homes Fund will prioritise households experiencing severe fuel poverty, including those with long-term health conditions, or disabilities.
answer_text: As part of the Warm Healthy Homes Strategy consultation, views were sought on the policy intent for the Warm Healthy Homes Fund. There was widespread agreement that the scheme should prioritise low-income and vulnerable households living in the poorest-performing properties, who are least able to pay for improvements themselves. This feedback has informed the final scheme design, which will be subject to a further public consultation in the coming months. In addition, the Warm Healthy Homes Strategy includes a commitment to strengthen the capacity of trusted partners, so that households seeking help are signposted to the full range of support available.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the current average waiting time for Violence Risk Assessment reports; (ii) the number of reports currently outstanding; (iii) how this compares with departmental targets; and (iv) any steps being taken to address delays, linked to staffing and capacity issues.
answer_text: As a result of the increasing prison population, the complexities of those within the population and limited resources within NIPS Psychology services, there have been some delays in the provision of violence risk assessments.  Targets for completion of assessments vary based on the circumstances of each case and NIPS Psychology Team aim to ensure risk assessment reports are produced within agreed timeframes to meet PCNI direction dates or other operational deadlines.  Over the past 12 months, where PCNI have directed Violence Risk Assessment completion within a set time frame; this deadline has been met in 61.39 % of cases.  A further 28.6 % were completed within an average of 2.5 months of the original deadline.  While 9.5% remain outstanding, these are due to be completed within the next 2-3 months.  NIPS have sought to mitigate delays to service provision through an external review of the Psychology Service; centralisation of waiting lists to ensure fair and equal access to resources; prioritisation of high-risk cases and those being reviewed by PCNI; the use of overtime; and the procurement of external support for the provision of violence risk assessments.  Currently, there are 148 referrals for individuals requiring a violence risk assessment across the prison estate.  The average time which these individuals have been on the waiting list for assessment is 9 months.  Over the next 12 months, 52 assessments have been directed by the Parole Commissioners for NI or are required for those within the parole process.  The average length of time these individuals have been on the waiting list for assessment is 5 ½ months.  Of those assessments currently allocated for completion, the average waiting time was 11 months.   An independent review of NIPS Psychology services has recently been completed, and the recommendations of this review, including in respect of staff recruitment and retention will be implemented over the next 18-24 months.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department intends to review current Disabled Facilities Grants funding arrangements and processing times, to ensure disabled people can receive the adaptations required to live safely and independently.
answer_text: I have been clear on the need to reduce processing times for Disabled Facilities Grants, and my Department is working with the Housing Executive and the Department of Health, as part of a wider review of NIHE Administered Private Sector Grants, to do so.  Our planned public consultation will be completed this year with recommendations to improve how grants are administered and improve user outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action his Department is taking to improve access to Disabled Facilities Grants, in light of ongoing delays in the provision of essential home adaptations.
answer_text: I recognise the importance of timely and accessible Disabled Facilities Grants (DFGs), particularly given the significant impact delays can have on individuals requiring essential home adaptations. I have been clear about the need to reduce processing times and improve access for DFGs.To that end, my Department continues to work closely with colleagues from the Housing Executive and the Department of Health as part of the wider review of NIHE Administered Private Sector Grants. This work focuses on streamlining processes and ensuring people can access the adaptations they need as quickly as possible.While progress has been made, work continues. I will share proposals for public consultation later this year.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of applications made to the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme, which are currently awaiting a determination.
answer_text: Since the Scheme opened in August 2021, more than 12,000 applications have been submitted with over £124 million paid to eligible applicants, offering meaningful recognition of the harm they have suffered. As of week commencing 9 February 2026, the Victims Payments Board have made determinations on 4,279 applications. There are 7,958 applications currently awaiting a determination.  Both the Executive Office and the Victims’ Payments Board continue to work closely with victims’ groups to keep the scheme under review and identify ways to increase the number of determinations. A recent review of operational effectiveness and performance has highlighted several opportunities to streamline processes and further improve the service provided to applicants.The Board is taking forward these recommendations through a dedicated Business Transformation Project Team, focused on providing a more efficient and responsive service to those engaging with the Scheme.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of the commitments on regional balance within the Programme for Government 2024-2027, to outline the steps being taken to ensure towns and rural areas will see measurable investment.

To ask the Minister of Health whether any concerns have been raised regarding geographical black spots in East Londonderry, where patients requiring a care package are unable to access one due to their address.
answer_text: The Northern and Western Trusts have provided the following responses:NHSCTWithin the Northern Trust, the East Londonderry area covers the old council area of Coleraine, and therefore includes Castlerock, Articlave, Macosquin and Garvagh.There are currently 87 service users waiting on a full or partial package of care in this area.  52% of these service users are awaiting a single call to be sourced. The geographical areas that can be challenging to provide a care package are Aghadowey, Castlerock and Articlave.  In addition where there are a small number of care hours required in a particular locality, this may deter independent homecare providers from increasing their capacity, in this locality. The Trust has in place an ambitious reform programme focusing upon the objectives of reducing unmet need, addressing rurality and levelling up provision of care across the Trust’s geographical area.WHSCTDemand for homecare services within the Trust continues to grow and currently exceeds what the Trust is resourced to deliver.  There are some areas within the constituency where there are particularly challenges in sourcing care packages.  This in the main is due to the challenges of rurality and workforce supply.  The wards of Banagher, Feeny, The Highlands and Upper Glenshane would fall into this category. The challenges associated with ensuring equitable provision across areas of rurality are not unique to the Western Trust.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether he will publish data on the number of applicants from different (i) localities; and (ii) backgrounds, who have progressed through each stage of the latest Civil Service recruitment drive for Administrative Officers in the north west.
answer_text: In line with all NICS external recruitment competitions, Section 75 data for the North West pilot AO competition will be published on the Open Data NI website.  This will be made public by 13 February 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any measures her Department is taking to address delays in the adoption of roads in housing developments that are preventing prospective homeowners from securing mortgages.
answer_text: On the 25th September 2025 I announced the introduction of the new Private Streets Enforcement Guidance in an effort to reduce the risk of developments entering the Department’s backlog of unadopted private streets sites. The guidance establishes a threshold for the initiation of enforcement action of 2 years following 80% occupation of houses in a development constructed from the 1st January 2024.My Department has also published new developer information which amends the bond release thresholds. This change will enable my Department to retain a larger proportion of the bond which provides the funds required to bring new developments’ roads infrastructure to an adoptable standard should enforcement action be necessary.The new guidance, implemented alongside the changes to the bond release thresholds, will protect homeowners by greatly reducing the risk of new developments remaining unadopted.For those developments commenced prior to 1st January 2024, the Department may also consider enforcement when a developer has defaulted through the issuing of a Notice under Article 11 of the Private Streets (NI) Order 1980, outlining the outstanding work, and allowing the developer to take action towards completion of the site within 28 days.  The developer has a right to appeal under Article 12.In instances where there is non-compliance with the Article 11 Notice, my Department may take forward the necessary works and recover the costs under the provisions of Article 15 and adopt the road on completion of the work.If the Developer does not pay costs as detailed in Department's request or the developer is no longer trading, the Article 15 demand is served to the appropriate Bank or Insurance Company acting as surety for payment of costs incurred in bringing the development to an adoptable standard.If a site is not brought to an adoptable standard by the developer, and the funds in the bond are insufficient to complete the infrastructure to an adoptable standard, my Department is not funded to carry out such works. My Department will continue to communicate with and encourage developers to have sites brought to an adoptable standard at the earliest date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 37518/22-27, for his assessment of what additional staffing or skill mix changes are required in Emergency Departments to support improved ambulance handover times.
answer_text: To help alleviate pressures in emergency departments (EDs), my Department has implemented a range of targeted initiatives utilising the skills of emergency nurse practitioners, advanced nurse practitioners and allied health professionals. This includes the expansion of urgent care centres, minor injury units, Same Day Emergency Care and ambulatory pathways across a range of specialties. In addition, Phone First services are now operational in all Trust areas, delivering a telephone clinical triage service to direct patients to the most suitable care pathway. These initiatives aim to reduce pressure on EDs and improve the overall experience for patients.  In 2025, my Department worked with the Royal College of Emergency Medicine and Trusts to ensure that an additional 23 emergency medicine consultants were appointed to work in EDs across Northern Ireland (a further seven consultants will also be recruited in the coming months). These additional ED consultants provide further resilience in the ED teams and extend senior decision-making into evenings and weekends.In EDs, ‘Frailty at the Front Door’ services are in place to improve the experience and health outcomes of older people attending hospital, as well as easing handover pressures by assisting with flow.  When patients arrive at hospital, either by ambulance or other means, Front Door Frailty Services focus on identifying older people with frailty, resulting in early comprehensive geriatric assessment (CGA).  CGA ensures that patients are diverted to the most appropriate services within the hospital as quickly as possible and, where possible, discharged home on the same day.  This prevents unnecessary hospital admission and reduces demand on hospital beds.With regard to expediting handover of patients from ambulance to ED, Hospital Ambulance Liaison Officers (HALOs) have a key interface role in ensuring good communication between the ambulance service and the hospital. In the largest ED sites (Royal Victoria, Ulster, Antrim, Craigavon and Altnagelvin Hospitals) NIAS provides HALO cover Monday to Friday from 7.00am to 11.00pm (16 hours) and either 7.00 am to 7.00pm or 9.00am to 9.00pm (12 hours) on Saturdays and Sundays. HALOs located at these sites also provide oversight of smaller sites in each of the respective Trusts. There will be periods where, from an operational perspective, NIAS is not always able to have a HALO presence on the hospital sites. When this happens, NIAS will work to provide other cover through station managers and the NIAS Control Room. NIAS and Trusts also join daily calls seven days a week with the Regional Control Centre to manage any interface issues and deal with these in a timely manner.NIAS is reducing conveyances to ED through the Integrated Clinical Hub (ICH). Its highly skilled senior clinicians can assist NIAS crews in establishing the best course of action and pathway for patients’ needs. NIAS is also working closely with Trusts to develop alternative care pat

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any measures his Department has put in place to ensure that Universal Credit texts and letters, issued as part of the transition from Employment and Support Allowance are accessible for adults with a learning disability, including the use of plain language and formats designed to minimise distress and confusion.
answer_text: I refer the member to my answer to AQW 38176/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the reason some Universal Credit claimants are still being asked to complete an HC1 form, for help with transport costs for medical appointments, following the reintroduction of automatic passporting to the Help with Health Costs scheme.
answer_text: Whilst the Department of Health introduced new legislation regarding the Help with Health Costs scheme which came into effect on 1 December 2025 this did not provide automatic passporting to the scheme for all Universal Credit claimants. Automatic passporting will apply to Universal Credit recipients who in their last assessment period had:  •	No earnings or•	Net earnings of £435 or less or•	Net earnings of £935 or less, and the assessment includes a child element, limited capability for work or limited capability for work and work-related activity. Those who are in receipt of Universal Credit, but are outside of the thresholds for automatic passporting, can continue to apply for Help with Health Costs by completing a HC1 form to have their eligibility assessed and confirmed prior to treatment. Further information is available at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/help-health-costs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the number of trees that have been planted around Lough Neagh in order to reduce nitrate run off in the last three years.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 37981/22-27. to provide a detailed timeline for when the Integrated Consultancy Team will be appointed and the Outline Business Case 2 for the New Kitchen and Café Project is expected to be completed.
answer_text: NIPS continue to engage with CPD to progress an Integrated Consultant Team procurement, with an indicative date for procurement preparation to be completed end April 2026 and contract awarded January 2027. The output from the Integrated Consultant Team will inform the Outline Business Case 2, which is expected to be submitted February 2028.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 37981/22-27, to detail any assessment her Department has made of the impact on catering operations at Magilligan Prison, during the period in which the new kitchen and café facilities are being developed.
answer_text: Based on the current designs and programming of works, it is not expected that there will be an impact on the existing catering operations at Magilligan prison during the period in which the new kitchen and café facilities are being developed. The new kitchen and café facilities will be developed on an alternative site closer to the prison population.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any discussions she has had with the Minister for Infrastructure to ensure that rural tourism areas, where visitor traffic is high, have reliable and modern Electric Vehicle chargers.
To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the phased redevelopment of the kitchen facilities at Magilligan Prison.
answer_text: A tender package is currently being prepared for an Integrated Consultancy Team to inform an Outline Business Case 2 for the New Kitchen and Café Project.  This project is part of essential capital works at Magilligan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on her Department resourcing an operator to manage the electric vehicle charge points at the new Northern Regional College Coleraine campus.
answer_text: My officials have consulted with Northern Regional College’s Facilities & Operational Development team and have been advised that a suitable economic operator has been appointed to manage the electric vehicle charge points at its Coleraine campus. It is anticipated that charge points will become operational at the Coleraine campus by the end of January 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how his Department is implementing the Ending Homelessness Together Homelessness Strategy 2022-2027 objective of addressing homelessness by providing settled, appropriate accommodation and support, in the Causeway Coast and Glens Council area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive chair quarterly Homelessness Local Area Group meetings in the Causeway Coast and Glens area which support the delivery of the Homelessness Strategy 2022-27. The membership consists of a range of local, statutory, community and voluntary partners, and provides the opportunity to ensure appropriate support is provided to households who experiencing or threatened with homelessness.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any change he expects in the level of homelessness in the Causeway Coast and Glens Council area during the remainder of the Ending Homelessness Together Homelessness Strategy 2022-2027 period.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive does not predict the level of homelessness. The focus is to prioritise homelessness prevention and work with statutory and voluntary partners to minimise the number of households who present as homeless in areas such as Causeway Coast and Glens.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any support available to help local businesses install workplace electric vehicle charge points.
answer_text: The provision and operation of EV charging infrastructure is a commercially led activity. The Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV) offers a range of grants to support EV infrastructure and my officials regularly engage with its officials on grants and funding opportunities for EV owners in the North. Further detail on available grants  can be found online at: Office for Zero Emission Vehicles - GOV.UK. Workplace charging support guidance is also available online at:  https://www.gov.uk/guidance/electric-vehicle-chargepoint-and-infrastucture-grant-guidance-for-installers. Additionally, the Electric Vehicle Association NI (EVANI) has prepared a helpful resource: Making Workplace EV Charging a Reality: A Guide – EVANI

To ask the Minister of Health to detail ambulance response times in the Causeway Hospital area, in each of the last 24 months, broken down by clinical category.
answer_text: Ambulance call categorisation for emergency calls is standardised across the UK.  For your information, ambulance call category definitions and response time standards are set out in Table 1 below. Improving handover times is therefore a key priority for my Department.Earlier last year, my officials, together with clinicians from the Health and Social Care Trusts and NIAS, visited a hospital in London to learn from their successful approach to releasing ambulances more quickly. Following that visit, the Trusts and NIAS were asked to adopt a similar model.In August 2025, a regional workshop involving The Trusts and NIAS drew on best practice from the London Ambulance Service and King George Hospital. Work is also underway to reduce shorter delays of 15, 30, and 60 minutes, with the longer-term objective of achieving the 15-minute standard.This is a system-wide challenge, and my Department is committed to leading the co-ordinated action required to deliver these improvements. question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the role the Public Protection Arrangements Northern Ireland currently has in securing suitable housing for offenders under its supervision; and (ii) the steps her Department is taking to ensure accommodation supports public safety and rehabilitation.
answer_text: The Public Protection Arrangements Northern Ireland (PPANI) bring together agencies including the Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI), the Probation Board for Northern Ireland (PBNI), the Health and Social Care Trusts and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive (NIHE) to collaboratively assess and manage the risk posed by certain sexual and violent offenders.     These agencies take into account a range of factors when assessing suitability of accommodation for such offenders which include: whether there are children or vulnerable adults living at or frequently visiting the address; whether the accommodation is in immediate proximity to the victim’s or child centred locations; and whether there are concerns about association with other offenders in the area. Guidance has been developed to ensure all agencies deploy a consistent approach to approving where these offenders may reside. My Department recognises that suitable accommodation can support public safety.  As such, my Department keeps the statutory guidance governing PPANI under review, including to ensure that operational agencies maintain their focus on public protection when considering where offenders should be housed. My Department also chairs the PPANI Strategic Management Board, at which it ensures that accommodation related issues are regularly reviewed and considered with those agencies which have operational responsibility for risk management and accommodation. My Department is also clear that securing suitable accommodation for offenders leaving prison is a critical factor in rehabilitating prisoners and reducing recidivism.  In December 2023, the Northern Ireland Prison Service (NIPS) published The Protocol for the Management of the Accommodation and Related Support Needs of People in Custody in Northern Ireland which sets out a multi-agency approach involving NIHE, PBNI and NIPS have taken forward a range of initiatives to support this. NIPS has completed a pilot which placed NIHE Housing Advisors within Maghaberry Prison. This provides early intervention on housing needs and encourages decisions on accommodation to take cognisance of other resettlement factors including health and employability. Early indications of the evaluation show it has been positive for the outcomes for prisoners; consideration is being given to expanding it to Magilligan and Hydebank.NIPS and PBNI work in partnership to utilise Approved Premises across Northern Ireland.  Approved Premises help rehabilitate and resettle some of the most serious offenders leaving custody whilst also monitoring and supervising residents diligently in the interests of protecting the public.  PBNI also manages offenders subject to statutory supervision in the community, such as those released on licence.  As part of the risk management plan, PBNI must approve where such offenders reside for the duration of the licence period.  Relevant cases will also be considered by PPANI agencies where the offender is subject to PPA

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any criteria used as part of the Housing Selection Scheme to assess whether a tenant is suitable for placement in an over 55's housing scheme.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the information requested from the Chief Executive who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Selection Scheme does not currently contain any specific criteria to assess whether an applicant is suitable for an allocation in an over 55s housing scheme. The Scheme does make provision for considering an applicant’s eligibility for all social housing. Once eligible the provisions in the General Rule (47) apply to allocations in most cases. This states an applicant is a relevant applicant if they have applied for accommodation in the area and with the characteristics of the property in question and the non-locational characteristics of the dwelling meet (and do not substantially surpass) the applicant’s needs.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any immediate steps his Department is taking to establish a safe and workable pathway for the movement and sale of animals vaccinated against bluetongue, in order to prevent financial losses for farmers.
answer_text: Vaccinated animals are currently not exempt from the movement restrictions as a result of the current Temporary Control Zones (TCZ).		Details on the impacts of movements as a result of the TCZ can be found at https://www.daera-ni.gov.uk/articles/bluetongue-situation-northern-ireland 		Restrictions will remain in place whilst the epidemiological investigation takes place.  This is of paramount importance, to ascertain if the virus has affected any further premises.  		My officials are working in close collaboration with their counterparts in the Republic of Ireland and across the United Kingdom on next steps, which will include reviewing movement controls, subject to legislative requirements as the situation develops.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he will answer AQW 33907/22-27.
answer_text: AQW 33907/22-27 was answered on 10 December 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, in relation to the current border closures due to the bluetongue outbreak, to detail why vaccinated livestock is prohibited from being moved to Scotland and England.
answer_text: Moves of susceptible species to Great Britain were temporarily suspended following confirmation of a Bluetongue outbreak in Bangor, Co. Down.After investigations and initial surveillance testing in the affected area were completed, the authorities in Great Britain confirmed that trade could resume for animals located outside the temporary control zone from 9 December 2025.The conditions for accepting live animals from Northern Ireland are determined by the authorities in Great Britain, based on their assessment of any potential risk to herds and flocks there. These conditions include decisions on whether vaccinated animals may enter Great Britain.DAERA continues to work closely with counterparts in Great Britain to ensure that trade from the Bluetongue Control Zone can safely resume, in line with disease control requirements required by Great Britain authorities.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on remedial work on Kilrea Bridge.
answer_text: Kilrea Bridge was closed on 20 September in the interest of public safety due to significant cracking on the retaining wall at the south-west end of the bridge.  I fully recognise the importance of Kilrea Bridge to the local community and wider transport network and apologises for the disruption this closure has caused.My officials have worked at pace to develop a solution and procure a specialist contractor who then started on 10 November.Works have progressed well and I am pleased to confirm that the temporary stabilisation work is complete that allowed cars and light vehicles over the bridge from last Saturday.  Completion of the permanent repairs to allow the bridge to be opened to all vehicles remains on target in mid February 2026, subject to suitable weather conditions.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail how they will ensure that regional campuses, such as Ulster University, Coleraine, are adequately considered within the multi-year budget process, to support balanced economic growth across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Regional balance is one of my four economic priorities, and I am committed to ensuring all Higher Education Institution campuses across the north remain thriving and sustainable centres of learning which deliver the appropriate skills needs for the region and support economic growth.My Department included future year Capital and Resource bids on behalf of the Higher Education sector, including Ulster University Coleraine, in the recent multi-year Spending Review budget exercise.  The Executive will decide the outcome of the Spending Review exercise in due course.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of schools taking part in the Reading with AI Research Project led by Oxford Brookes University, using Amira Learning, to improve reading in schools; and (ii) whether the results of the research will be used by his Department to inform future practice.
answer_text: As of 2 December 2025, there are 114 schools participating in the Reading with AI Research Project.The project provides an opportunity to test how AI tools can support literacy, aligned with research-informed approaches to reading.  A research report will be provided to my Department by Oxford Brookes University at the close of the project and there may be opportunities for the results of this research to inform future practice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the road closure schedule to enable work to be completed at Kilrea Bridge, Bann Road, Kilrea.
answer_text: Work on the bridge continues to progress well, and we were able to reopen the road to cars and light vehicles (up to 3.5 tonnes) in early December, following the completion of the temporary stabilisation work.  Permanent repair works are also being progressed, and subject to favourable weather, are expected to finish by mid-February 2026, as originally planned (14 weeks from 10 November). Once complete, the road will fully reopen to all vehicles. My Department will continue to provide regular updates and maintain the dedicated web page to keep the public informed of progress and key milestones via Kilrea Bridge, B64 Bann Road, Kilrea – Closure | Department for Infrastructure, and our TrafficWatchNI website via https://www.trafficwatchni.com/twni/.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has given any consideration to a (i) reduced speed limit; and (ii) school crossing warning sign, outside the new Mill Strand Integrated School and Nursery site at Ballywillan Road, Portrush.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.School warning signage and enhanced 30mph speed limit signage has been provided at this location.  ‘BUS STOP’ and ‘SCHOOL KEEP CLEAR’ road markings have been installed in an effort to improve safety at school pick-up and drop-off times. A footway link at the junction of Girona Avenue and Ballywillan Road has also been provided to enhance opportunities for active travel.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North which will bring the total delivered since 2020/21 to 273 schools https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/20mph-speed-limit-schools-tranche-3.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.While the Tranche 3 programme focuses on schools on rural roads where the national speed limit applies, I am aware that there are schools in urban and residential areas which would also benefit from a reduction in speed limits. My officials are carrying out a review on a number of speed related matters including options for permanent 20mph zones in residential areas and near schools and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise in the near future as part of this review.  Further consideration on the use of part time speed limits at schools will be taken on completion of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any steps being taken to support the growth of student numbers at Ulster University Coleraine campus.
answer_text: Regional balance is one of my four economic priorities, and I am committed to ensuring all Higher Education Institution campuses across the north remain accessible, thriving and sustainable centres of learning. I strongly support Ulster University’s delivery of Higher Education in Derry, Belfast and Coleraine. My Department works in partnership with the university to deliver investment across all its campuses, including Coleraine, ensuring the appropriate skills needs for the region continue to be met. In response to the Magee Taskforce ask, an independent assessment of the additional costs associated with the university’s multi-campus model is also underway.In recent years Ulster University has expanded its academic offering at Coleraine with a range of new undergraduate and postgraduate courses. I am pleased to confirm that there has been a notable increase in student numbers. During Academic Year 2024/25, the number of enrolments at the campus increased by over 200 to approximately 3,300 students. Further growth is anticipated in 2025/26.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure early identification of self-neglect in older adults.
answer_text: Self-neglect is one of the key challenges in the care of the elderly, particularly where individuals have the mental capacity to refuse support.Historically, self-neglect has differed from other forms of abuse because it does not involve a perpetrator, which has meant it has been hard to define and it is viewed differently from other types of abuse, neglect or exploitation.  Under the extant Adult Safeguarding and Protection Policies and Procedures in Northern Ireland, self-neglect is not included as a category of abuse.    In response to the consultation on the new draft Adult Protection Bill, the Commissioner for Older People has raised concern that self-neglect is excluded from the draft Bill, given that self-directed harm and self- neglect have been included in adult safeguarding legislation in other UK  jurisdictions, namely Scotland and England.   My Department are planning to meet with the Commissioner in the near future to give this matter further consideration.The Primary Care Multi-Disciplinary and Community Social Work teams in the HSCTs, along with stakeholders amongst the Community and Voluntary sector have been pro-active in addressing self-neglect among older adults.  My Department oversees a number of contracts with the Community and Voluntary sector that support older people to live well with long term health conditions by providing emotional and practical supports and which recognise of the need to address potential self-neglect amongst older people and to tackle social isolation.As you are aware my Department is moving forward with the Neighbourhood model for Primary, Community and Social Care which will deliver greater levels of care for citizens in their communities and will involve a focus upon early intervention and a preventative model of care.  This model will see Community Pharmacy, GPs and their Federations, Voluntary and Community Organisations, Trusts, Independent Providers, other Statutory bodies and Local Government working closely together in formal partnership to provide integrated care to improve the health and wellbeing of people living in their neighbourhood.  My Department is currently involved in the planning and build phase with a plan to move to implementation by the beginning of April 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will consider removing the Causeway Hospital from the Northern Health and Social Care Trust and aligning it with the Western Health and Social Care Trust to help improve healthcare for residents in the north east and north west of Northern Ireland.
answer_text: As outlined in my Department’s strategic plans, including the 3 Year Plan and the Reset Plan, the wider HSC system must be configured to provide optimal outcomes and experiences for patients. One of my key priorities was to launch a public consultation on ‘Hospitals – Creating a Network for Better Outcomes’. The aim of the document is to describe our hospital system and provide a strategic context for future hospital reconfiguration decisions. At its heart, this work is about how we can improve sustainability of hospital services and ultimately provide an assurance that none of our acute hospitals will close. The Hospital Network framework is underpinned by a number of existing service reviews and strategies which were clinician led and developed in partnership with those who use the services. A fundamental concept is that of an integrated Northern Ireland hospital network. It is about ensuring effective use of hospital space and resources to meet population health needs and maximise positive outcomes for patients. The principle of local provision where possible, and central provision where necessary is key in the document. Changes to our hospitals will be led by the policy or operational owners at either regional or HSC Trust level and include detailed financial investment requirements, projected productivity gains and ensure we are treating in the right place, at the right time, by the right person. I have been clear that every hospital is needed and I have no plans to consider moving Causeway Hospital to the Western Health and Social Care Trust. Moving one hospital from one Trust to another would require considerable planning and have implications for services, patients and staff. Simply put it would be a distraction with no material patient benefit. My priority at the moment must remain delivering tangible improvement in patient outcomes. Causeway Hospital will continue to be an important part of the regional Northern Ireland network of hospitals, working collaboratively across HSC Trust organisational boundaries. This will ensure the health system can provide sustainable, optimal care to patients for all of the population, including those residents in the north-east and north-west of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline any plans he has to adjust future funding allocations to grow capacity at Ulster University Coleraine campus.
answer_text: I intend to bring forward recommendations for a multi-year Budget to the Executive next month. This will give the Executive a chance to examine the investment required for all bids as part of its wider Budget consideration.    It will then be for the Executive, collectively, to agree how funding is allocated across departments.For capital spending, the Budget will provide a four-year funding envelope. This will provide departments with certainty to begin to plan how to meet their priorities over the budget period.It would be a matter for the Economy Minister to determine how any funding provided by the Executive is allocated, working alongside the universities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether any additional funding streams are available for clubs that were unsuccessful in securing support through the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: I understand the need and challenges that football clubs face and that is why I have committed to delivering the Northern Ireland Football Fund. I acknowledge that the need exceeds the current budget, and I will continue to seek additional funding for future years to meet the full demand.  I am determined that clubs not selected in the prioritised cohort will be brought forward to a further stage of the Programme if additional funding can be secured. However, I understand the uncertainty faced Clubs.  They may wish to contact Sport NI who can inform them of any funding opportunities that may be relevant. In addition, the Northern Ireland Council for VoluntaryAction ( NICVA) operate a very useful Grant Tracker https://www.grant-tracker.org/ which has been designed to help charities, community groups, and voluntary organisations find and manage funding opportunities relevant to their projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the stakeholders involved in the establishment of Causeway Academy, Coleraine; and (ii) how often this body meets.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has confirmed that an Interim Board of Governors was established on 9 November 2023 to take forward the establishment of Causeway Academy. The Interim Board of Governors is made up of 18 members with equal representation (six members) from each of the current Boards of Governors of Coleraine College, Dunluce School and North Coast Integrated College. Ulster University have a co-opted representative (non-voting) as a member.   The Interim Board of Governors works in partnership with the EA, as the managing authority for the new controlled integrated post primary school, and is supported by the Northern Ireland Council for Integrated Education and Controlled Schools’ Support Council, all of whom are represented on the Interim Board of Governors.  The Principal Designate is the secretary to the Interim Board of Governors.The Interim Board of Governors normally meets monthly (business permitting) and since its establishment on 9 November 2023 has had 24 meetings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any meetings (i) that have been held; or (ii) scheduled to be held, with the Ulster University in relation to possible sites for Causeway Academy, given that the University is a partner in the new school.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that its Area Planning officers have met with Ulster University (UU) representatives on the following occasions –7 October 2020Discussion on and commitment from UU that UU would work in partnership with the new school, regardless of site.13 January 2021Exploration of the possibility of a UU site for the school, with all due diligence, including child protection and safeguards to be considered. 17 March 2024Exploration of viable options for a school on the UU site. 29 May 2025Discussion relating to strategic partnerships with UUIt should also be noted that the UU has a co-opted representative (non-voting) on Causeway Academy’s Interim Board of Governors.   The UU representative is a regular attendee at Interim Board of Governors meetings since its establishment on 9 November 2023.Causeway Academy has not been included in a Major Works announcement and while initial discussions may have taken place with UU about site options, the preferred site will ultimately be identified through the business case process, should the school be announced for major capital investment in the future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the timetable for the Construction and Procurement Delivery feasibility studies for Civil Service buildings at (i) County Hall, Coleraine; and (ii) Crown Buildings, Coleraine.
answer_text: Feasibility studies encompassing County Hall and Crown Buildings are currently ongoing to assess the future accommodation requirement in the Coleraine area. These studies will be used to inform an appropriate Business Case which is planned for completion by the end of June 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail how (i) staff; (ii) trade unions; and (iii) local stakeholders, will be consulted if the decision is taken to consolidate Civil Service staff into one building, in Coleraine.
answer_text: Officials in my department have been engaging with accommodation representatives of affected departments to develop options for a Business Case to assess the future Civil Service accommodation requirement in the Coleraine area. My Officials have informed Central Trade Union Side, as appropriate, of the ongoing project and will support occupying departments who are responsible for consulting with staff and local Trade Union representatives. Consultations and further communications will continue as the project progresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any discussions her Department has had with other departments, in relation to the introduction of a support scheme for traders affected by the long-term closure of the Bann Bridge, Kilrea.
answer_text: I fully appreciate the impact of the ongoing closure and the understandable frustration of the local community, including businesses in the area and thank them for their patience while we work to deliver a safe and effective solution. I want to reassure you that we are working at pace to strengthen the road and will continue to explore every opportunity to accelerate progress and minimise disruption.My Department regrets any inconvenience experienced by the local community and businesses due to the works and associated road closure. My Department, however, does not offer compensation to businesses for pure economic loss resulting from the exercise of its statutory duty to maintain the public road networkI am pleased to confirm that the appointed specialist contractor commenced work to repair the damage on Monday 10 November. Whilst the 14 week programme is longer than originally anticipated due to the difficult ground conditions on site, my officials continue to engage with the contractor to explore options to reduce the timescale and minimise disruption. We will keep the local communities and elected representatives up to date on the timeline throughout the works. My Department will continue to provide updates on the repair works on our website at: Kilrea Bridge, B64 Bann Road, Kilrea – Closure.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the projected headcount of Civil Service staff working in Coleraine over the next ten years; and (ii) any assessment made on whether consolidation of staff into one building would mean a reduction in local jobs, or services.
answer_text: My officials are currently consulting with departments that occupy our buildings in the Coleraine area to understand their future accommodation requirements, including the number of staff working from this location. This work will inform the development of options that will be assessed through the Business Case process to identify the optimal accommodation solution and inform any decisions.The consolidation of Civil Service staff into one building would allow for better, more efficient use of accommodation and would not mean a reduction in local jobs or services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the rationale as to why further education college staff, employed on National Joint Council terms and conditions, were excluded from the Education Authority's recent pay and grading review, regarding the increase to Grades three and four pay.
answer_text: The Education Authority are not the employers of Further Education college staff employed on National Joint Council terms and conditions, therefore the recent pay and grading review undertaken by the Education Authority does not apply to Further Education staff.  I am advised by the Further Education colleges, who are the relevant employers of further education staff employed on National Joint Council terms and conditions, that the National Joint Council pay agreement for the period 2018 to 2020 included the introduction of a new pay spine from 1 April 2019. The Further Education Colleges undertook their own review of pay and grading at that time and implemented a new pay spine in April 2019 following extensive negotiations with recognised trade unions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department is taking to ensure National Joint Council-aligned further education staff are not disadvantaged in future pay reviews or regrading exercises.
answer_text: My Department has responsibility for approving pay reviews and regrading exercises for National Joint Council-aligned Further Education staff under Public Sector Pay and Pay Remits approval policy. However, the responsibility for the agreement and implementation of pay reviews and regrading for staff lies with the Further Education colleges, as employers. I have been advised by Further Education colleges, as employers, that they have and will continue to implement National Joint Council pay reviews and regrading exercises as detailed in any future approved pay negotiations.
