question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any steps her Department will take to ensure that rural and coastal communities, like the Causeway Coast and Glens local council area, receives the fair share of funding from Invest NI.
answer_text: My department and Invest NI are firmly committed to delivering greater levels of investment to all parts of the North. In line with this commitment, my Sub-Regional Economic Plan has been created as a framework to improve sub regional economic performance.Invest NI published its new strategy last year entitled ‘Our Future in Focus’ (Invest NI Business Strategy 2024-2027 (PDF)). This strategy is directly aligned with my department’s commitment to balanced regional growth. To deliver against this strategy, Invest NI is implementing a new regional approach that is seeing it increasing the capacity and capability across its regional office network, assisting it to seek out, identify, and engage with more companies than ever before. To measure its success, Invest NI has set ambitious targets, including a new target to increase the number of investments outside the Belfast Metropolitan Area to 65% by the final year of their Business Strategy, 2026/27. Over the previous 5 years the average was c.56%. This is one of the first steps in a long-term commitment to better shape economic performance right across the North to provide better outcomes for all our people. Alongside this, with support from Invest NI and my department, all 11 Councils have established Local Economic Partnerships. These Partnerships have brought together central government, the business community, universities and colleges, local enterprise agencies, and civil society to identify the main barriers to economic development and the priority interventions that will build the region’s value proposition to help deliver improved sub regional delivery. My department are supporting the Local Economic Partnerships with dedicated funding of £45 million over the next three years.The approach has been welcomed by the Council and Economic Development Partners in the Causeway Coast & Glens Council area who have been proactively working closely with my department and Invest NI in the development of a local action plan for the Council area which will be launched shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail how the recent extension of magistrates’ sentencing powers, now allowing up to 12 months imprisonment for a single offence, has impacted (i) case throughput; and (ii) Crown Court backlogs, since its implementation in November 2024.
answer_text: Given the need for a balance between timely information and making the reoffending rate as meaningful as possible, Official Statistics for reoffending are measured and reported using a one year proven reoffending rate across Northern Ireland and, more widely, across Great Britain. The Department does not routinely report on reoffending beyond this methodological approach.With the above in mind, we have used data from the two most recent cohorts which can be used to calculate estimated reoffending rates beyond the one-year observation period for those released from custody or given a non-custodial disposal in 2020/21 and 2021/22. Estimated reoffending rates for these individuals are detailed below.Table 1: Adult reoffending rates beyond the one-year observation period for the 2020/21 and 2021/22 cohortsYearAdults in cohortAdults who reoffended within one yearAdultone-year proven reoffending rate (%)Adults who reoffended within two yearsAdulttwo-year proven reoffending rate (%)Adults who reoffended within three yearsAdult three-year proven reoffending rate (%)2020/2114,9232,75818.5%4,59530.8%5,55737.2%2021/2218,3353,18417.4%5,52830.1%N/AN/ATable 2: Youth reoffending rates beyond the one-year observation period for the 2020/21 and 2021/22 cohortsYearYouths in cohortYouths who reoffended within one yearYouthone-year proven reoffending rate (%)Youths who reoffended within two yearsYouthtwo-year proven reoffending rate (%)Youths who reoffended within three yearsYouth three-year proven reoffending rate (%)2020/2170817725.0%26637.6%31344.2%2021/2286120223.5%34239.7%N/AN/ANote:An offence is counted as a reoffence if (i) it occurs within the given period following custody release or non-custodial disposal, (ii) it has been committed in Northern Ireland, (iii) it has been prosecuted via PSNI and not a third party, (iv) the offence is not a breach offence, and (v) the offence has been ‘proven’, meaning that a court conviction or diversionary disposal has been imposed.Adults are defined as those aged 18 over and youths are defined as those aged 17 and under on release from custody or receipt of the disposal which led to inclusion in the annual cohort.Differences in the offending related characteristics of those included in each cohort make comparing reoffending rates problematic, across cohorts and jurisdictions.My Department continues to work on multiple fronts to address and reduce reoffending rates. This process requires buy-in and coordination across multiple policy areas, including many that rest with other Executive departments. I must also highlight that one of the most effective ways of reducing reoffending is the use of a range of appropriate sentencing options which facilitate meaningful rehabilitation, including in the community. The pursuit of lower reoffending rates would be greatly aided if all stakeholders were committed to that evidence-based approach, and it is therefore regrettable that some choose to focus instead on the misguided and unsub

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people housed temporarily, through the Mears Group, who have been re-housed, within the Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council area via the Housing Selection Scheme, in each year, since the Mears Group were awarded the contract for assisting with accommodation for asylum seekers.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from its Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Any households who present as homeless to the Housing Executive directly from Home Office accommodation (provided by Mears Group in Northern Ireland) upon an asylum decision being reached will be recorded under the homelessness reason No Accommodation in Northern Ireland: Section 95 which has been in effect since July 2023. In such circumstances, the Housing Executive can only provide figures since July 2023 and not the start of the Mears contract in 2019. Since July 2023 there have been zero allocations of social housing with Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council to households who presented as homeless where the reason for presentation was No Accommodation in Northern Ireland: Section 95.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) how the High Court prioritises bail and trial hearings for individuals suspected of reoffending while awaiting trial; (ii) the specific measures her Department and the judiciary are taking to reduce the backlog of such cases; and (iii) any assessment her Department has made of any risks posed to the public during these delays.
answer_text: (i)  The listing and management of bail and trial hearings is a matter for the independent judiciary.    The Lady Chief Justice’s Office has published guidance and practice directions to ensure that criminal cases are dealt with efficiently and expeditiously.  These are available on the Judiciary NI website (https://www.judiciaryni.uk/).  This includes specific guidance on the listing of High Court bail hearings and a Practice Direction on Case Management in the Crown Court which outlines the obligations on participants and the factors which need to be taken into consideration before a case can progress to trial.  (ii) The timely progression of criminal cases is central to the effective delivery of justice and delays can have significant consequences for victims, defendants, and for public confidence in the system. Tackling delay is one of the biggest challenges facing the criminal justice system and is one of my key priorities. My Department and justice colleagues are committed to reform the way in which criminal cases are handled, and are currently engaged in a programme of work targeted at reducing avoidable delay across the system. This work is driven by the Criminal Justice Board and takes forward existing work on Committal Reform, existing and emerging work in the digital arena and explores other areas for efficiencies such as Early Engagement between key stakeholders to help drive case progression, increased sentencing powers for magistrates’ courts and expanding the use of Out of Court Disposals. My Department has also recently been successful in a bid to secure Transformation funding which will help to drive forward work in relation to Early Engagement and Out of Court Disposals.Taken together, these reforms aim to rebalance the justice system, ensuring a more proportionate approach to offending, and freeing up capacity to focus on the most serious cases. I recognise there is more we need to do to improve case processing times, and remain committed, alongside my justice partners, to driving forward the changes needed.(iii) The granting of bail and the setting of bail conditions are a matter for the independent judiciary and will be based on the individual circumstances of the case.  There is a general right to bail for all persons accused of offences or awaiting trial.  When deciding whether or not to grant bail a judge is required to take into consideration a range of factors which may include risk that the accused will fail to appear for trial, interference with the course of justice, prevention of further offences and preservation of public order.  During an application for bail the defence and prosecution will make representations to the court on whether bail should be granted, and what conditions if any are necessary to manage the risks involved.  The monitoring of bail conditions is a matter for the police.   Where a person does not comply with their bail conditions, the police can arrest them for breach of bail, and the prosecutio

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps her Department is taking to address the frequent cancellation of Translink bus services across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink continues to meet its service reliability targets and has operated 99.31% of scheduled Metro mileage and 99.63% of scheduled Ulsterbus mileage from April 2025. Translink recognise that there may be occasions when it has had to cancel services due to operational challenges including external service impacting issues outside of its control such as road works, traffic congestion, demonstrations and adverse weather. Translink monitor its service performance on a daily basis to ensure service reliability is maximised and in turn meets the expectations of its customers. In addition Translink continues to work with officials from my Department to increase bus priority measures in our towns and cities which in turn will further improve the reliability of services. For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 31850/22-27, to outline (i) what contingency plans are in place to support patients who may face delays or disruptions in care due to the ongoing collective action by GPs; and (ii) how his Department intends to monitor and respond to any emerging risks to patient safety or service access.
answer_text: As you are aware, my Department has written to GPs to remind them of their responsibilities on these issues and will closely monitor adherence to ensure that practices continue to deliver to their contractual requirements.GPs must continue to deliver upon their contractual commitments as they take this action and ensure that impact on patients is minimised. Any action that is taken must respect contractual obligations, protect patient safety, and maintain service access.  Any practice specific delays or issues should, in the first instance, be raised directly with the practice.As outlined in my response to AQW 31850-22-27 my Department will ensure that GP Practices continue to meet their contractual obligations through a number of methods such as:Review of information submitted through the Northern Ireland Contractual Adherence Framework (NICAF) 2025/26GP Practice VisitsMonitoring of complaints received through ‘honest broker’ serviceLocal offices will also continue to work with GP Practices to ensure all required information is provided.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for an update on the proposal for a Veterinary School at Ulster University's Coleraine campus; and (ii) to detail any recent discussions her Department has had with Ulster University and Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs.
answer_text: My Department has not received a proposal for the establishment of a veterinary school. Should a specific proposal be put forward, I am open to working in partnership with all relevant stakeholders to consider the implications.  Any proposal would, of course, need to be considered by all those who have stake in the delivery of veterinary medicine here including Executive colleagues, the universities and relevant professional bodies.  My Department has not had any recent discussions with Ulster University or the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs regarding a proposal for a veterinary school here.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline why the electric vehicle charge points are not operational at the new Northern Regional College site, Coleraine.
answer_text: My officials have consulted with Northern Regional College’s Facilities & Operational Development team who have advised that, following installation of the electric vehicle charge points at its Causeway (Coleraine) Campus in March 2025, the units were taken offline until a suitable operator could be appointed to effectively manage the units’ operational and maintenance service. Following the completion of a recent public sector procurement exercise, it is anticipated that the charge points will become operational towards the end of November 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will consider introducing a temporary blue badge for applicants who need short-term help, when their condition is expected to improve within a year.
answer_text: The eligibility criteria for the Blue Badge Scheme includes those with a permanent disability where a person cannot walk or has considerable difficulty walking and also covers those with difficulty in planning and following a journey as it would cause overwhelming psychological distress. The eligibility criteria can be viewed at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/blue-badge-eligibility-criteria and there are currently no plans to amend this to include short-term medical conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given the significant rise in benefit overpayments, to detail any specific measures his Department is taking to reduce official error.
answer_text: The latest estimates published in the Departments Annual Report & Accounts show that benefit overpayments due to official error are 0.4% of overall benefit expenditure.    My Department is committed to reducing official error and has an extensive programme of work in place to ensure benefit payments are accurate and cases are corrected at the earliest opportunity, if appropriate. This includes dedicated quality checks carried out by operational benefit branches. Feedback from these checks is disseminated to staff and bespoke training is delivered, where necessary.    I have repeatedly bid for additional resources to support greater investment in fraud and error activities, and I continue to make the case to Executive colleagues for this funding. In my statement to the Assembly on 11 February 2025, I announced the establishment of a Task and Finish Group to critically examine every intervention taken regarding benefit fraud and error. The findings of this Group will inform any additional measures which may be put in place to address official error.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the schedule for the maintenance of cycle lanes within and Causeway Coast and Glens local council area.
answer_text: My Department considers cycle lanes in the same way as footways for inspection and repair purposes unless they are part of the carriageway.  In these instances, the carriageway criteria for inspection and repair are adopted.My Department has well established policies for maintenance standards, intervention levels and inspection frequencies.  These policies are provided in the links below and are applied consistently across my Department, including in the Causeway Coast and Glens council area.https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/road-maintenance-standards-for-safety-revised-inspection-frequencies-dem-158-24.pdfhttps://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/road-maintenance-standards-for-safety-limited-service-in-2021-22-dem-160-21.pdf

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the timescale, from application to completion stage, for a disabled parking bay application.
answer_text: My Department’s powers to provide parking places are contained in Articles 10 and 13 of the Road Traffic Regulation (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 with comprehensive guidance regarding the application criteria for disabled parking bays provided in RSPPG E056: Provision of Parking Places RSPPG E056.Applications for disabled parking bays are generally processed within six months, provided there are no objections. However, this timeframe may vary depending on staff availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given that input costs such as fertiliser and fuel are putting pressure on farm incomes, to outline what targeted support his Department is considering to prevent further closures of family farms.
answer_text: While overall, agricultural market conditions have been generally positive in recent times, I acknowledge that volatility in prices and input costs, due mainly to world markets which are outside our control, remain a difficult issue for farmers to address.  To focus effort on helping farmers mitigate cost and price pressures, my Department is providing support through the Sustainable Agriculture Programme (SAP) to help farm businesses enhance their productivity and strengthen their resilience while also improving their environmental sustainability. The SAP encompasses a wide range of schemes, and I would encourage all farmers to avail of the support. Further information on the SAP is available on the Department’s website.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) to outline the expected timeframe for GP practices to provide medical notes to legal firms upon receipt of a valid consent form; and (ii) whether any measures are in place to ensure timely compliance with data protection legislation.
answer_text: General Practices in Northern Ireland, as Data Controllers, are expected to provide medical notes in accordance with the time frames outlined in the UK General Data Protection Regulations (UK GDPR) and the Data Protection Act 2018.  Typically, such requests for information will be processed under the framework of a Subject Access Request (SAR). The response time for Subject Access Requests is one calendar month from receipt of the request and valid consent. This timeframe can be extended by a further two months in instances where a request is deemed complex or numerous, in such instances the requester should be informed within the first month and given a reason for the delay.Where compliance with Data Protection legislation is a concern it is recommended this would be raised initially via the individual GP Practice’s internal complaints procedure. Where such concerns remain, these can be directed to the Information Commissioners Office. The Information Commissioner's Office (ICO) is the UK's independent regulator responsible for enforcing data protection and information rights laws. The ICO has a range of regulatory powers at its disposal which can be considered where there is failure by an organisation to comply with the legal requirements outlined in both the UK GDPR and Data Protection Act 2018.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline any mitigations being considered to reduce the financial burden on families, particularly in relation to transport, uniforms, and additional costs, arising from the continued use of a split-campus model for Causeway Academy.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that it is working with the Principal Designate of Causeway Academy on the configuration of sites for opening the new school on 1 September 2026. The Principal Designate has been working with the Interim Board of Governors, the Principals of the three schools and the EA to ensure that everything is in place for pupils and staff in relation to curriculum, transition planning and branding of the new school.  In terms of the costs of school uniforms, it is not anticipated that there will be any additional financial support available to families attending the new Causeway College when it opens in September over and above that available to all low-income families through the EA's uniform grant scheme. I introduced the School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill which, while it is not in operation until such time as it may pass Final Stage and receive Royal Assent, provides an opportunity for the Causeway College to review and design its uniform policy in consultation with its parents and pupils to address the issues of affordability, comfort and practicality in a way that does not generate an undue financial burden.  Transport arrangements for pupils attending Causeway Academy will be considered further once final arrangements for school sites and year groups have been finalised. Once confirmed the EA Transport service will engage with the school and relevant transport providers to confirm transport arrangements for the scheduled school opening in September 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the timeline regarding site selection, planning, and construction of the new Causeway Academy; and (ii) whether local stakeholders will be consulted before key decisions are made.
answer_text: The new Causeway Academy will open in September 2026 across three existing sites. The Education Authority is currently working with the Principal Designate on the configuration of the three sites.   A communications plan is in place to provide updates to current and prospective pupils, parents, staff, governors and the wider school community at key times. 	Whilst a new school formed and operating on a split site following an amalgamation would be well placed to receive funding for a new build in a future major capital call, there can be no guarantees that this would be the case. Given the constrained capital budget position, I cannot confirm when I will be in a position to make a new call for major works projects. I will continue to make the case for increased and sustained investment in education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether the Education Authority has submitted, or intends to submit, a formal business case for a new post-primary school building in Coleraine following the amalgamation of North Coast Integrated College, Dunluce School, and Coleraine College.
answer_text: Causeway Academy will open in September 2026 across three existing sites. The Education Authority is currently working with the Principal Designate on the configuration of the three sites.   The indicative capital investment required to establish the new school on the three sites will be calculated once final planning is completed in the coming weeks.	Whilst a new school formed and operating on a split site following an amalgamation would be well placed to receive funding for a new build in a future major capital call, there can be no guarantees that this would be the case. Given the constrained capital budget position, I cannot confirm when I will be in a position to make a new call for major works projects. I will continue to make the case for increased and sustained investment in education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the criteria used to prioritise cases in the High Court.
answer_text: Each of the three divisions of the High Court, Chancery, Kings Bench and Family, manages a wide range of court business which are governed by legislation. court rules, as well as guidance, protocols and practice directions published by the Lady Chief Justice’s Office.The listing and management of cases is a matter for the independent judiciary.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline (i) the rationale for individuals awaiting trial who are suspected of continuing criminal activity, but can remain at large; and (ii) the steps her Department is taking to address these risks, while also ensuring fairness in the judicial process.
answer_text: (i)  The granting of bail and the setting of bail conditions are a matter for the independent judiciary and will be based on the individual circumstances of the case.  There is a general right to bail for all persons accused of offences or awaiting trial.  When deciding whether or not to grant bail the judge is required to take into consideration a range of factors.  The grounds on which bail may be refused fall into four main categories: • Risk that the accused will fail to appear for trial; • Interference with the course of justice;• Prevention of further offences; and• The preservation of public order.During an application for bail the defence and prosecution will make representations to the court on whether bail should be granted, and what conditions if any are necessary.  (ii) The monitoring of bail conditions is a matter for the police.   Where a person does not comply with their bail conditions, the police can arrest them for breach of bail and the prosecution can apply to the court for bail to be revoked.Should a person be convicted of a crime that was committed while on bail, the Criminal Justice (Northern Ireland) Order 1996 states that, when considering the seriousness of the offence, the court shall treat the fact that it was committed in those circumstances as an aggravating factor.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on progress being made to improve the A37 route in East Londonderry.
answer_text: My Department completed a substantial £737,000 resurfacing scheme on the A37 near Limavady in August 2024.Plans for a climbing lane on the A37 at Gortcorbies near Limavady were developed over ten years ago. However, the scheme did not proceed due to constraints in available funding and resources at that time. This proposal will now be reconsidered as part of the new Regional Strategic Transport Plan 2035.As part of this process, strategic transport schemes are being reviewed and prioritised in alignment with the objectives of the new Regional Strategic Transport Plan.At this stage, no decisions have been made in relation to the Plan. I would welcome all feedback once the plan has been published for consultation which is currently scheduled for Spring 2026.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any action their Department is taking to ensure that departments, particularly the Department for the Economy, deliver on regional balance commitments as set out in the Programme for Government 2024-2027.
answer_text: The PfG commits to tackle economic disparities and promote regional balance.We recognise that NI can only truly thrive and succeed if all parts are reaching their potential.  We have welcomed a number of recent announcements in the North West, attending the opening of Ernst and Young office at Ebrington, Alchemy Technology Services and the significant investment in Seagate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how his Department will monitor GP practices to ensure contractual obligations and patient safety standards are maintained during the period of collective action.
answer_text: I regret that it was not possible to secure agreement with the NI General Practitioners Committee on this year’s contract and their subsequent decision to take collective action.  The 2025/26 GMS contract included an additional £9.5m investment in General Practice. In the context of the inadequate budget allocated to my Department by the Executive, and the extremely challenging financial position the Department faces, this represented the best possible offer that could be made.  In addition to the 2025/26 GMS contract, my Department has announced investment of a further £61m into the Primary Care MDT programme over the next 5 years, which will expand the range of services available in Primary Care and help address the high levels of demand being dealt with by our GP practices, as well as £2.9m into the GP Elective Service which will increase the levels of service being provided in important primary care services including the Gynae service. I have also accepted pay recommendations for 2025/26 from the relevant bodies and have issued a Ministerial Direction instructing their immediate implementation. The Ministerial Direction has been referred to the Executive for approval. Taken together, total investment in General Practice in 2025/26 would be £414m.My priority has been, and continues to be, to ensure that patients can continue to access safe, high quality, sustainable GP services when they need to.  This continues to apply during collective action.  GPs must continue to deliver upon their contractual commitments as they take this action and ensure that impact on patients is minimised. Any action that is taken must respect contractual obligations, protect patient safety, and maintain service access. My Department has written to GPs to remind them of their responsibilities on these issues and will closely monitor adherence to ensure that practices continue to deliver to their contractual requirements.  Departmental colleagues will ensure that GP Practices continue to meet their contractual obligations through a number of methods for example:Review of information submitted through the Northern Ireland Contractual Adherence Framework (NICAF) 2025/26GP Practice VisitsMonitoring of complaints received through ‘honest broker’ serviceLocal offices will also continue to work with GP Practices to ensure all required information is provided.In terms of access to GP services more widely, the General Medical Services (GMS) contract for 2025/26, introduced a range of measures which, while relatively small in scale, will make a tangible improvement to the patient experience of accessing GP services.  These measures do not seek to increase the number of consultations delivered nor to make GPs work to unsafe levels.  Rather, they aim to create small but significant improvement to the patient experience of access, to remove some of the friction points for patients that can make the experience worse for patients and practices alike. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 27941/22-27.

To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to outline any factors, in addition to the published application scoring system, applied in determining which clubs progressed through the Northern Ireland Football Fund process for stadia funding; and (ii) to publish (a) the scoring breakdown, (b) any additional criteria; and (c) the rationale, applied in each case.
answer_text: No additional factors were applied in determining which clubs progressed through to the next stage of the programme. Each applicant was scored  and ranked within their tier solely on the information provided in their application against the published scoring criteria. When deciding how many projects to take forward from each tier in rank order, I considered a number of options presented by my officials. Based on this advice, I opted to take forward a range of small, medium and large projects to ensure that the available budget can be utilised to maximise benefits for football and communities and avoid any further erosion of its value. 	I will not be publishing the information requested at this stage. The Department will fully consider requests for any information made in line with terms of the relevant legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any financial incentives or support schemes available to encourage local businesses to take on trade apprentices.
answer_text: Apprenticeships play a key role in delivering my vision for the Economy – providing good jobs right across the north. I am proud of the continued growth and success of my Department’s apprenticeship programmes, with trades such as electrotechnical, engineering and construction among the most popular apprenticeship frameworks.My Department funds 100% of the cost of directed off-the-job training for apprenticeships with annual funding of approximately £45 million. This ensures employers can focus on providing practical, on-the-job experience. Businesses are eligible for an employer incentive payment upon the successful achievement of Level 2 and Level 3 apprenticeship frameworks, ranging from £670 to £2,008 depending on the occupational area. In addition, the Construction Industry Training Board (CITB NI), offers an Employer Apprentice Grant to further support employers in the construction sector.I want apprenticeships to go from strength to strength – I recently published an Apprenticeship Action Plan which aims to expand the talent pipeline for employers and increase access to apprenticeship opportunities for individuals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 30242/22-27, to detail (i) the procedure to recall a bond from a developer to carry out works, under the Private Streets (Northern Ireland) Order 1980 and the Private Streets (Amendment) Order 1992; and (ii) the number of times her Department has recalled a bond from a developer to complete works, over the last three years.
answer_text: The department may consider enforcement when a developer has defaulted. The following is a summary of the process. The bond is held by a surety and the costs incurred by the department are recovered from the surety rather than the developer.A Notice under Article 11 is served in writing to the Developer /Responsible person who is named in the Bond, and copied to the bond holder. The Notice will outline the outstanding work, and allows the developer to take action towards completion of the site within 28 days The developer has a right to appeal under Article 12.Subsequently, Under Article 13 where a notice under Article 11 is not complied with, the Department may, a) execute the street works b) Recover its expenses in accordance with the provisions of Article 15; and c) declare the street to be a public road from the date of completion of the works. If there is no appeal by Developer as responsible person, the department would then survey and design the necessary remedial work. The department will then appoint a contractor to complete the works to the required adoptable standard. A notice under Article 15 is served to recover expenses is served by the Department on the responsible person. A detailed breakdown of costs accompanies the demand.If the Developer does not pay costs as detailed in Department's request. or the developer is no longer trading, the Article 15 demand is served to the appropriate Bank or Insurance Company acting as surety for payment of costs incurred in completing the development to an adoptable standard.In the past 3 years, my Department has recalled a bond from a developer to complete works under the Private Streets Order on 34 occasions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQO 2312/22-27, to detail the communication strategy in place to provide information about possible site changes associated with the new Causeway Academy to (i) parents of current pupils; and (ii) parents of pupils currently in Primary 7, who will be selecting secondary schools.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA), as proposer of the Development Proposal to establish the new school, has a statutory obligation to proceed with its implementation. The EA has advised that a Causeway Academy Newsletter has been issued to staff and parents, providing milestone updates for the new Causeway Academy.     A Communications Plan is in place to provide updates to current and prospective pupils, parents, staff, governors and the wider school community at key times. The EA is working with the Principal Designate on the configuration of sites for opening the new school on 1 September 2026.  When this has been confirmed, the Principal Designate will issue relevant communications, by newsletter and social media.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when the new MRI scanner at the Causeway Hospital will be fully operational.
answer_text: As part of the Northern Ireland Rapid Diagnosis Centre investment, an MRI scanner has recently been installed in Causeway Hospital. MRI is a highly specialised area of medical imaging, using sophisticated equipment and operated by highly trained diagnostic staff.New MRI scanners are required to undergo a thorough and detailed commissioning process in order to ensure they are safe and effective. In comparison to other capital schemes, MRI scanners have added requirements in relation to significant estates works, electrical supply and calibration of the equipment. As part of the normal procurement process, the Causeway Hospital scanner requires rigorous testing prior to clinical use and Northern Trust staff are undertaking necessary steps to ensure that a safe and effective diagnostic service can be delivered to patients. The Northern Trust have confirmed the scanner will be operational shortly. The Causeway Hospital MRI scanner is funded for 10 sessions of MRI from the rapid diagnosis centre funding for regional cancer pathways. This funding is being prioritised to improve cancer waiting times and patient outcomes. The regional funded capacity on the Causeway MRI scanner is approximately 4,000 examinations per year.The expansion of imaging capacity at the regional rapid diagnosis centres requires a regional approach to ensure all patients have equal access to this service across Trust boundaries, negating the risk of postcode lottery.  Therefore, the project is being taken forward in collaboration with the Regional Medical Imaging Board, who are progressing with implementation of the Strategic Framework for Imaging Services in NI 2018) and the NI Cancer Network.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) how his Department is supporting patients affected by the withdrawal of GP services such as ambulance bookings, blood tests and benefit paperwork as part of the ongoing contract dispute; and (ii) whether there is a plan to transferer these responsibilities to other parts of the health service.
answer_text: I regret that it was not possible to secure agreement with the NI General Practitioners Committee on this year’s contract and their subsequent decision to take collective action.  The 2025/26 GMS contract included an additional £9.5m investment in General Practice. In the context of the inadequate budget allocated to my Department by the Executive, and the extremely challenging financial position the Department faces, this represented the best possible offer that could be made.  In addition to the 2025/26 GMS contract, my Department has announced investment of a further £61m into the Primary Care MDT programme over the next 5 years, which will expand the range of services available in Primary Care and help address the high levels of demand being dealt with by our GP practices, as well as £2.9m into the GP Elective Service which will increase the levels of service being provided in important primary care services including the Gynae service. I have also accepted pay recommendations for 2025/26 from the relevant bodies and have issued a Ministerial Direction instructing their immediate implementation. The Ministerial Direction has been referred to the Executive for approval. Taken together, total investment in General Practice in 2025/26 would be £414m.My priority has been, and continues to be, to ensure that patients can continue to access safe, high quality, sustainable GP services when they need to.  This continues to apply during collective action.  GPs must continue to deliver upon their contractual commitments as they take this action and ensure that impact on patients is minimised. Any action that is taken must respect contractual obligations, protect patient safety, and maintain service access. My Department has written to GPs to remind them of their responsibilities on these issues and will closely monitor adherence to ensure that practices continue to deliver to their contractual requirements. In terms of access to GP services more widely, the General Medical Services (GMS) contract for 2025/26, introduced a range of measures which, while relatively small in scale, will make a tangible improvement to the patient experience of accessing GP services.  These measures do not seek to increase the number of consultations delivered nor to make GPs work to unsafe levels.  Rather, they aim to create small but significant improvement to the patient experience of access, to remove some of the friction points for patients that can make the experience worse for patients and practices alike.  In many cases, these are things that practices here are already doing and the aim has been to seek to spread that good practice across the region.Looking to the future of General Practice, I am committed to working with GPs on the future of primary care services and that we continue to engage constructively with General Practice.  My Department has invited NIGPC to engage in formal negotiations to develop a new GMS contract for 2026/27, with the aim of helping sta

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) how will the expansion of the ambulatory care pathway at Causeway Hospital be resourced; and (ii) what impact will it have on Emergency Department pressures.
answer_text: The Causeway Acute Ambulatory Unit (CAAU) opened on 3 April 2024 and is operational 9am-5pm, Monday to Friday, exclusive of bank holidays.  There are currently no plans for any expansion of the CAAU, however this continues to be monitored by the Medicine and Emergency Medicine Team in the Northern HSC Trust. Any expansion would however be dependent on capacity in the unit and availability of funding.During 2024/25*, 2,853 patients were managed through the CAAU, who in most cases would have otherwise received treatment in the Emergency Department. During this time, this activity resulted in an average of 175 patients avoiding hospital admissions per month.* November 2024 activity unavailable due to Encompass system launch

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what incentives are in place to encourage those in under-occupied social housing properties to apply for a transfer.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows.“The Housing Executive does not currently offer financial or other direct incentives for tenants to transfer to alternative accommodation in the social sector.However, tenants can request a transfer or participate in a mutual exchange if they wish to move to a different property.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the options available to homeowners in new developments, where builders have refused to bring roads and footpaths up to a safe and acceptable standards.
answer_text: All new developments are subject to the Private Streets (NI) Order 1980 and the Private Streets (Amendment) Order 1992, which require developers to enter into an agreement with the Department to provide the roads, footways and sewers to the Department’s standards. This agreement is secured by a bond, that may be used by my department to complete the road works should the developer default.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions that (i) have been taken; and (ii) will be taken to limit future algae blooms in the River Bann.
answer_text: The River Bann (Lower) flows directly from Lough Neagh, and the presence of blue-green algae downstream is linked to algal blooms occurring within the Lough. Consequently, actions taken to address blue-green algae in Lough Neagh should be the focus of attention and will yield positive outcomes for water quality in the River Bann. 	The Lough Neagh Report and Action Plan, agreed by the Executive in July 2024, contains science led actions to tackle the blue-green algae blooms in Lough Neagh and secure longer-term improvements in water quality across Northern Ireland. Of the 37 actions included in the plan, 14 have been completed, 22 are currently underway, and 1 is pending due to its reliance on another action being implemented.I remain fully committed to driving forward implementation of the Action Plan, working closely with other Departments, particularly the Department of Infrastructure and the Department of Justice, where their input is required.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given that NHS guidance and Health and Social Care Trust policies state that clinical uniforms and scrubs should not be worn in public areas outside healthcare facilities, (i) why do staff go into public spaces in their scrubs; and (ii) what steps his Department is taking to ensure consistent enforcement of these infection prevention measures.
answer_text: (i) All HSC Trusts have confirmed that a uniform dress code policy is in place and that all staff who wear clinical uniforms or scrubs are expected to strictly adhere to these policies. Staff are required to comply with Health & Safety legislation to minimise the risk of infection to themselves, patients, and visitors. While there is no evidence of an infection risk from travelling or shopping in uniform, the policy advises that staff should, where possible, change into and out of uniform at their workplace.The Department issued a Regional Dress Code Policy and Recommendations on Staff Changing Facilities for Northern Ireland on 19 February 2008. This regional policy forms the basis of each Trust’s local policy, which is reviewed and updated annually.Promote a professional image and public confidence.Support infection control efforts.Address health, safety, legal, and security considerations.Recommend staff changing facilities.Staff are regularly reminded to use workplace changing areas and avoid public activities in uniform. Community staff may make essential purchases (e.g. lunch, petrol) while in uniform. For public-facing duties, uniforms should be covered unless visibility is therapeutically necessary. Uniforms must be covered on public transport.(ii) Trusts run regular awareness campaigns and audits to ensure staff understand and follow dress code policies. The Trust Board and Chief Executive hold overall responsibility for ensuring that arrangements are in place to enable all staff to comply with the policy and ensure that systems are in place to do so, including adequate uniform supply.Senior managers are accountable for implementing the policy, monitoring compliance, and providing evidence of adherence. They must also take appropriate action when staff fail to comply without approval.Managers are expected to be flexible where staff have medical, religious, or disability-related needs. Staff unable to comply should discuss this with their line manager.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the rationale behind the decision to introduce a comfort dog into Magilligan Prison; and (ii) any safeguarding measures in place to ensure the welfare of the animal.
answer_text: (i)	In prisons, the presence of emotional support dogs can offer an additional way to support people with poor mental health and addiction issues, help promote positive changes in behaviour and reduce stress.  Support dogs can also help prisoners enhance their social skills, self-esteem, and self-confidence.  These benefits can contribute to reducing the risk that someone might re-offend and prevent further victims of crime in the future.The welfare of this animal is Magilligan’s primary concern. He is never left alone  with prisoners and is under continuous supervision by staff. Before his arrival, a full risk assessment was carried out, and any prisoner with a history of offences involving animals is excluded from all contact with him.  The Governor of Magilligan recently invited animal welfare officers from Causeway Coast and Glens Council to visit the prison and view Bailey.  Their independent report is available on the Department’s website, and they confirmed that Bailey’s living conditions and care are appropriate and that he is a happy, contented dog and in excellent condition.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any new technologies are being used to detect and track algae blooms in the River Bann.
answer_text: The River Bann (Lower) flows directly from Lough Neagh, and the presence of blue-green algae downstream is linked to algal blooms occurring within the Lough. Consequently, actions taken to address blue-green algae in Lough Neagh and its catchment should be the focus of attention and will yield positive outcomes for water quality in the River Bann. 		Within Lough Neagh and its catchment, the Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) continues to conduct monthly sampling for chemical and phytoplankton analysis alongside the operation of three telemetered buoys and six in-situ monitoring locations, which collect physico-chemical data. These strategically placed systems support trend analysis and provide early warning of bloom development and movement.Seaton’s Marina on the Lower Bann is part of the North Coast Bathing Waters program, which includes weekly monitoring for blue-green algae. Additionally, the Department is collaborating with the UK Space Agency on a Small Business Research Initiative (SBRI) to develop a remote sensing solution for detecting, monitoring, and predicting blue-green algae. Phase 2 of this initiative began in September 2025 with results expected in April 2026. Initially focused on Lough Neagh, the project aims to expand to other associated water bodies in the future.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how the recently announced multi-site arrangement for the new Causeway Academy will impact pupils and staff during the interim period.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised that Causeway Academy will open in September 2026, across three existing sites, with differing student numbers on each site. This approach is student centred, with the view of minimising movement for students throughout their post primary school career, maximising the educational experience and achievement of all students, whilst allowing exam year groups to complete their education on their current sites. Students will also benefit from a familiar environment, understanding established routines, relationships, and support systems on their current site. Staff can also concentrate on exam preparation and pastoral care without the challenges of transition.The Day 1 plan also supports staff making the transition into a new school, with careful planning to minimise travel between sites. This will allow for exposure to new courses and year groups that they may not have previously been involved in. The interim Principal has been working with the Interim Board of Governors, the Principals of the three schools and the Education Authority to ensure that everything is in place for pupils and staff in relation to curriculum, transition planning and branding of the new school.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail what legacy plans are in place to ensure maximum economic benefit following the success of The 153rd Open at Royal Portrush.
answer_text: Tourism NI, on behalf of my Department, is committed to working with partners to deliver on the legacy benefits from The 153rd Open. The 153rd Open is forecasted to generate over £150 million of positive media coverage for the North, showcasing worldwide not only the compelling offering of our links golf, but also the beauty of our landscapes, the warmth of our welcome, exceptional visitor experience and our ability to successfully host major events. Such positive coverage will allow us to attract increasing numbers of visitors here in future years, fuelling the growth of tourism revenue towards our £2 billion revenue target from overnight visitors by 2035.Tourism NI will continue to use the successful hosting of The 153rd Open to promote golf tourism here, attracting high spending golf visitors.  To ensure the opportunity is fully maximised, Tourism NI has a golf tourism strategy in place to grow the value of golf tourism to £85 million a year by the end of the decade, and we are currently on track to achieve this figure. We have already seen significant investment in infrastructure since hosting The Open in 2019 including the Dunluce Lodge and Marine and Lawn properties. Investment from Galgorm Group into a new links golf course in Bellarena was announced in July, which will deliver tourism benefits and offer additional capacity in the North for visitors seeking the links golf product.   Focus on the US market for golf tourism will remain a priority. As part of the legacy plans, The Golf in Ireland tri-agency partnership (Tourism NI, Fáilte Ireland and Tourism Ireland) will continue to promote the destination internationally.  However, hosting The 153rd Open not only drives growth in golf tourism; it also provides an opportunity for promotion of the wider destination. Tourism NI will continue to work with Tourism Ireland, international media and tour operators to increase international tourism revenue in line with the 10 Year Tourism Vision and Action Plan. Invest NI also used the event to host potential international investors, highlighting the north as an attractive place to invest and work and they have continued to engage with these businesses following the Open to maximise its impact. The Open delivered 3,000 local jobs and over 1000 volunteering opportunities. Skills development benefits gained will strengthen our workforce here in the North for years to come.  A dedicated legacy fund for projects benefiting the local community and the environment is currently being delivered and will focus on sustainability, low-carbon technologies, circular economy, nature conservation, and enhancing social value.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the weed spraying of footpaths and roadsides in East Londonderry.
answer_text: My Department undertakes routine weed control on the adopted road network, including footways, throughout the North.  Weed control is undertaken annually and normally results in weeds initially being sprayed once per year.  Further treatments are undertaken if required, following an assessment of the effectiveness of the initial action.Within the East Derry constituency, weed treatment commenced on 5 May 2025 and is currently ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the (i) frequency; and (ii) cleaning schedule of road signs.
answer_text: My Department carries out regular inspections of all public roads and footways to ensure that essential response maintenance is identified and completed as necessary. During these inspections all defects are noted, including for example defective signs and signs requiring cleaning to improve their visibility.However, my Department’s resource budget, which is used to deliver day-to-day maintenance activities has been under significant pressure for over a decade. In response, my Department has had to reduce its routine road maintenance activities to a limited service, which prioritises delivery of essential services for maintaining public safety. Defective, damaged or missing signs, as well as those needing to be cleaned, are categorised as urgent or non-urgent work with priority given to mandatory and warning signs. Cleaning of other signs is carried out as permitted by availability of resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the specific split site locations being considered for the new Causeway Academy.
answer_text: The establishment of the new Causeway Academy is a complex undertaking and while I understand that there is strong support among stakeholders for a single-site solution for the new school, the Department must ensure that the preferred option aligns with the Department’s capital funding parameters, existing commitments and established processes.There are four potential sites - the three existing school sites at Coleraine College, Dunluce School and North Coast Integrated College, as well as the vacated former Coleraine High School site at Lodge Road, Coleraine.I have asked the Education Authority (EA) to review the business case to ensure that it appropriately considers all potential options, including utilising all three existing school sites; using various combinations of two of the four available sites; as well as single site operation on any of the four potential sites. This review is ongoing and my officials continue to engage with the EA to ensure that a suitable solution can be identified as soon as possible, to provide clarity to the schools and all stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how transportation will be managed for students and staff, during the interim phase of the new Causeway Academy school.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that transport arrangements for pupils attending Causeway Academy will be considered further once the location of the new school has been agreed. When the site, or sites, have been confirmed, the EA Transport Service will engage with the school and relevant transport providers to confirm transport arrangements for the scheduled school opening in September 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of drivers who failed to meet the visual acuity requirement and were permitted to drive under the special circumstances, in the last three years.
answer_text: There are no circumstances whereby a person who does not meet the visual acuity criteria would be permitted to hold a driving licence.  The visual acuity criteria, being the most basic form of visual standards for driving, are set out in regulations. There is no policy nor any special circumstances which waive the visual acuity standards.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the current number of individuals awaiting a medical driving licence assessment; and (ii) the average waiting times for medical licences in the last three years.
answer_text: As of 24 June 2025, 1988 drivers have had an initial assessment completed by the Driver and Vehicle Agency’s (DVA) medical officers and the DVA is awaiting further medical information to progress their applications. This may include information from their GPs or consultants, driving assessment reports or reports from DVA’s contracted specialist medical advisory service. The DVA does not hold information relating to the average waiting times for medical licences as the time taken to determine an application where medical assessment is required will depend on the nature of the medical condition and whether any further information or medical examination is required as part of the assessment process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the amount not recovered by his Department for healthcare provided to patients from the Republic of Ireland in the last three years.
answer_text: As with the rest of the UK, eligibility to access publicly funded healthcare in Northern Ireland is based on ordinary residence. A person will be ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland when that residence is lawful, voluntary, and for a settled purpose as part of the regular order of their life for the time being, whether of short or long duration. Anyone who is ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland is entitled to access publicly-funded health services. A visitor is any person who is not ordinarily resident in Northern Ireland. The Provision of Health Services to Persons Not Ordinarily Resident Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2015 (PNOR regulations) provide that health services shall also be made available to any visitor at a charge and detail those services and categories of visitors which are exempt from charge.All Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts have responsibility for establishing whether an individual seeking hospital treatment is eligible to access publicly-funded health services and each HSC Trust undertake their own ordinary residence assessments. If a patient does not meet the criteria of ordinary residence, HSC Trusts must establish whether the visitor or the services they are accessing fall within one of the exemptions set out in the PNOR regulations and should therefore not be charged. Where charges do apply, HSC Trusts must make and recover charges as appropriate. The Department is responsible for the legislation and associated guidance that provides authority to each individual HSC Trust to make healthcare charging determinations. Assessment of entitlement in accordance with the legislation and guidance, in conjunction with the eligibility circumstances outlined by the patient, is a matter for each individual HSC Trust. Information related to healthcare charging is held by each individual HSC Trust. To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has any plans to review the application criteria for disabled parking bays, which currently state that there cannot be a convenient parking place within 25 meters of the applicant’s front door or main household access.
answer_text: My Department’s Roads Policy & Procedure Guide E056 identifies the assessment process for individuals seeking an accessible parking bay close to their home. To ask the Minister of Finance to confirm what the revised completion date for payments to former Northern Ireland Prison Service members affected by the McCloud judgment will be.
answer_text: Whilst there is no legislative requirement for the completion of all payments once affected members have made their remedy choice, my Department anticipates it will be in a position to commence processing returned choices in September 2025. This process will continue until all Remedial Service Statements have been issued and choices are returned. Members have one year to make their choice, therefore the end date will be determined by when the last choices are received or due and the volume of rectification required. Returned Remedial Service Choices will generally be processed in order of receipt across all affected scheme members rather than by an individual’s former employment type.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail, further to the statutory requirement under section 80 of the Public Service Pensions & Judicial Offices Act 2022, why Civil Service Pensions (NI) will not meet the 31 March 2025 deadline to issue Remediable Service Statements to former Northern Ireland Prison Service members affected by the McCloud judgment.
answer_text: The statutory requirement for issue of Remediable Service Statements to all Civil Service Pension Scheme members (including former Prison Officers) affected by the McCloud judgment is contained at section 29 of the Public Service Pensions and Judicial Offices Act 2022. The full statutory requirement is for these to be provided by 31 March 2025 or ‘such later day as the scheme manager considers reasonable in all the circumstances in the case of a particular member or a particular class of member’. It has been necessary for Civil Service Pensions to use the flexibility contained at section 29 to complete this exercise to an extended timeframe. This is due to the volume and complexity of calculations involved in providing each eligible member with an individualised illustration of the available benefit options for their service during the period 1 April 2015 to 31 March 2022, which the McCloud remedy requires. Full completion of the exercise may take up to March 2027. Civil Service Pensions has written to all affected members to update them on the position and to direct them to additional information and detail on progress, which is being regularly updated on the Civil Service Pensions website at: https://www.finance-ni.gov.uk/articles/distribution-immediate-choice-remediable-service-statements-rssThe Pensions Regulator, Civil Service Pension Board, and NICS TUS have also been informed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on the remedial work at the Northern Ireland Housing Executive apartments at Ramore Street, Portrush, following Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. This engagement was in response to the roof damage sustained during the storm.Both the contractor and consultant have confirmed their commitment to completing the necessary remedial works. The contractor has indicated their intention to commence work as soon as possible. We are currently finalising the proposals and confirming a start date. Once this is agreed, we will promptly inform all residents.’

To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the review of the Home to School Transport policy.
answer_text: Under the Home to School Transport Policy, pupils are eligible for transport assistance if they live at least three miles from their post primary school; and have formally sought, and were unable to gain, a place in any schools of the same category within three miles of their home.  Parents are not obliged to send their child to the nearest school in category, however if transport assistance is a priority then they must apply to all schools in their chosen category which are within three miles of home, regardless of whether they believe their child will secure a place. Where a school includes academic selection criteria, the minimum score which would secure a place is not known in advance as it is dependent on how many places are available and the number of pupils who apply.  A review of the Home to School Transport Policy commenced in 2018, however this was paused by the then Minister due to the pandemic and further suspended pending the outcome of The Independent Review of Education. The transport policy underpins other policies within my department such as school attendance and is a key support for many pupils with Special Educational Needs and Disabilities (SEND).  Any amendments to the transport eligibility criteria would need to complement the work of the SEN Reform Agenda and it is in this context that I will consider the utility and timing of a new separate review of the transport policy. Given the very significant budgetary pressures facing my department I will also need to consider the affordability of any changes to the transport policy that would result in the widening of the criteria to access transport provision. The cost of delivering the current school transport service for over 90,000 pupils is significant at over £120 million in 2023/24.
