question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline the impact that creating Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools will have on schools.
answer_text: Specialist provisions in mainstream schools are an important part of the continuum of education for children with special educational needs (SEN), providing a smaller targeted learning environment appropriate to the needs of the learners, whether that be social communication needs or learning difficulties. Schools report many benefits associated with the establishment of specialist provision for the whole school community, and in particular the children educated in them.  They afford children with SEN the opportunity to be educated alongside their peers and siblings, and support the integration and inclusion of all children, creating educational environments which reflect the wider communities in which children live, while supporting parental choice of sector and location as far as possible.  Schools report positive learning and experiences for all children, teachers, parents and governors alike.Schools receive financial support in the establishment of specialist provision which covers the running costs and staffing of the provision, as well as a capital payment of £20,000 for each new specialist provision, which was introduced this year following my announcement on the SEN capital programme and which has since April has funded almost £3m in furniture and equipment grants.  Furthermore, schools establishing new specialist provision receive intensive support from the Education Authority’s Specialist Setting Support team who offer a suite of learning resources and provide advice and guidance throughout that first year and beyond where required.  Given the unprecedented increase in the number of children with SEN, the creation of additional specialist provision is a critical priority and a key component of the future continuum of education for children with SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he has any plans to set up a task force to bring forward an action plan for expansion at Ulster University's Coleraine Campus.
To ask the Minister for Communities whether there is an obligation on Housing Executive tenants to have improvement scheme works completed in their properties.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The General Conditions of tenancy is an agreement between the tenant and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive which details rights and obligations on the part of the tenant and that of the Housing Executive. It is a legal contract between the two parties.   Part 1 (Tenants Obligations) - Clause 18 requires that the tenant must permit the Housing Executive or persons authorised by it to enter the dwelling for the purposes of:viewing its condition; orreading, installing, servicing, improving or removing meters; orcarrying out works of repair; orcarrying out any other works (whether of improvement, alteration or otherwise). In Part III of the General Conditions of Tenancy the Housing Executive and the Tenant mutually agree: Improvements 3. The Housing Executive has the right to carry out any works in or in respect of or in connection with the dwelling (whether works of repair alteration improvement internal or external decoration, or otherwise)Failure to allow access to the property is a breach of the express terms of the tenancy agreement and as such entitles the Housing Executive to obtain to a court order compelling tenants to provide access or for the Housing Executive to seek possession of the property.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of staff affected by the removal of the maternity department at Causeway Hospital; and (ii) the number of staff affected if emergency surgery no longer takes place at Causeway Hospital.
answer_text: (i) The Maternity department remains in Causeway Hospital providing antenatal and ambulatory services within the Causeway Maternity Hub.  Inpatient services and births only were transferred to the Antrim site in July 2023. Following reconfiguration of these services, 19 staff members (midwives, maternity support workers and health care assistants) transferred to the Antrim site, two staff members transferred to the Gynae ward in Causeway and a further two staff members transferred into Trustwide positions.  All staff involved were met with by members of the senior midwifery team, their employee representative and the human resource team and options of where they would like to work were offered and explored . All staff were facilitated with their first preference.(ii) There are approximately 93 surgical staff across both Antrim and Causeway sites incorporating Nursing and Medical staff.  The proposed option to deliver high volume elective surgery on the Causeway site and emergency surgery on the Antrim site means that services will need to be reconfigured to deliver this. Some staff on both sites may be impacted depending upon the final model and their preferred options around work and location. The Trust has an established management of change process and as part of this all staff impacted will have an opportunity to discuss their circumstances, their preferences and the impact on their Contract of Employment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his plans to retain staff who are not prepared to travel from their current place of work at Causeway Hospital if services are to move to Belfast or Antrim.
answer_text: My Officials have been in touch with colleagues in HR in the Northern Trust who have confirmed that if General Surgery services move from Causeway to Antrim, the Northern Trust has a well-established ‘Management of Change’ process which is used to ensure a consistent and fair approach to the implementation of transformational change and staff engagement. Sustaining safe services remains a priority for the Trust and the expectation is that staff will continue to work in their substantive specialty/service area. There may however be a change to the type and complexity of work undertaken. There may also be opportunities for some staff to redeploy to an alternative post to remain working in their current location in the Causeway Hospital.If the option agreed at the end of the current public consultation is likely to lead to a change of base/location or change any other significant contractual term, the Trust will work with staff on both a collective and individual basis to understand their concerns about the change and to mitigate impact where possible. Staff will have an opportunity to have 1-1 discussions during the management of change process to discuss and agree key terms and conditions issues in accordance with relevant employment legislation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there is a contingency plan for a patient who is too sick to travel from Coleraine to Antrim if emergency surgery no longer takes place at Causeway Hospital.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Specialist Transport and Retrieval Service (NISTAR) is already in place for stabilisation and safe transfer of critically ill neonatal, paediatric and adult patients. Local processes are also in place for patients who would not meet the NISTAR criteria for transfer.The pathway for this specific scenario will be addressed as part of the planning process for any service change although such a scenario is anticipated to be a rare occurrence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of mothers that have given birth in ambulances or cars since the removal of maternity services from Causeway Hospital in 2023.
answer_text: Since the amalgamation of inpatient maternity services and births in the Northern health and Social Care Trust on 17 July 2023, to date 20 women have experienced birth before arrival to hospital, four of whom were women who reside within the Causeway locality. They Trust do not hold specific information in relation to the location of the birth, or pertaining to birthing in a car or ambulance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to increase car parking spaces at Causeway Hospital.
answer_text: The Northern Health and Social Care Trust have advised that they currently have no plans in place to increase car parking spaces at Causeway Hospital however this will be kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the Department’s plans for County Hall, Coleraine.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the difficulties with the layout of County Hall in Coleraine which meant it was ruled out of the Department for Work and Pensions job expansion.
answer_text: For the expansion of the services delivered on behalf of the Department for Work and Pensions, sites were assessed based on available capacity and on their proximity to existing service centres to enable support to be provided from existing management teams, to help deliver on the expansion.In relation to available sites located in the East Londonderry constituency area, both Jubilee House, Ballykelly and the Library Building, County Hall, Coleraine were considered. Jubilee House, Ballykelly was chosen as the preferred option for the expansion, as it offered around 40% more capacity and was located closer to an existing service centre based at Lisahally, Strathfoyle.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any discussions that have taken place with the Ulster University with a view to increasing student numbers and expansion of the Coleraine campus.
answer_text: My Department maintains a dialogue with Ulster University on the full range of issues affecting its sustainability and growth across all of its campuses. I fully support and recognise the benefits of the University’s three-campus model in providing accessible higher education to students from all communities.There have not been specific discussions with Ulster University about increasing student numbers or expanding the campus at Coleraine.  I am open to engagement on how my Department can best continue to support the higher education sector as a whole across the North.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the £2.80 permitted for pupils on free school meals will be increased in line with the increase in prices in school canteens.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for determining the price of a school meal in the schools where it is responsible for the provision of catering. The EA has confirmed that while it is currently reviewing its price of a meal, no decision has yet been made. If its meal price is changed as a result of this review, the EA has confirmed that the same change would apply to the allowance provided to pupils entitled to free school meals in post-primary schools. This will ensure that free school meal pupils will always be able to access the hot meal of the day, theme counters and sandwich bars.  Each Board of Governors in a voluntary grammar or grant-maintained integrated school is responsible for the provision of school meals in its school. It is also responsible for determining its price of a school meal and the allowance it provides to pupils entitled to free school meals. This allowance must be sufficient to enable a free school meals pupil to access a minimum of one meal choice that is suitable as a pupil’s main meal of the day.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he has considered additional traffic management measures in Coleraine to accommodate the five schools that will be in close proximity following the amalgamation of two sites at Coleraine Grammar School.
answer_text: As part of the planning process for the relocation of Coleraine Grammar School, the Education Authority commissioned a Traffic Statement which was prepared by independent consultants.This Traffic Statement concluded that the existing roads infrastructure can accommodate safely the merging of Coleraine Grammar Lodge Road campus to its Castlerock Road campus.  This work was reviewed and accepted by my department in its role as a statutory consultee.I acknowledge that during the peak hours there is significant congestion on the Castlerock Road which is currently exacerbated by a bottleneck caused by the location of the four school bus stops. In relocating Coleraine Grammar School, these bus stops will be removed and relocated to the new campus in December 2024.Going forward departmental officials will monitor the area.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, as part of his efforts to address regional imbalance, to detail (i) any plans in place to increase student numbers at Ulster University Coleraine campus; and (ii) whether there are any further plans for expansion at the campus.
answer_text: I fully support and recognise the benefits of Ulster University’s three-campus model in providing accessible higher education to students from all communities.While the expansion of Ulster University’s Magee campus is a commitment in ‘New Decade, New Approach’ and a priority in the draft Programme for Government, my Department will continue to collaborate closely with the university to support the sustainability of all of its campuses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting time for patients who have been recalled for a biopsy following a routine breast screening, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: A key national standard within the Breast Screening Programme measures the proportion of women referred for assessment following a breast screening mammogram whose date of first offered appointment at an assessment centre is less than or equal to 3 weeks (less than or equal to 21 calendar days) from attendance for the screening mammogram. The acceptable level is greater than or equal to 95.0% and the achievable level is greater than or equal to 99.0%.The table below provides the reported performance for the period 1 April 2023 – 31st March 2024 and shows that all Trusts exceeded the acceptable threshold of 95% for this period: Trust% of women offered an appointment less than or equal to 3 weeksBelfast/ South Eastern HSC Trust98%Northern HSC Trust100%Southern HSC Trust100%Western HSC Trust97%Northern Ireland average99%

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) who is responsible for the safety of pupils on school transport; and (ii) how he can ensure that seat belts are available on all buses transporting school children.
answer_text: The Department of Education are responsible for Home to School Transport policy.Seat belts are available on home-to-school transport services provided by Translink. In addition, Translink comply with all legislation relevant to public transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the rationale for not installing seat belts in the new fleet of electric buses.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and been advised as follows: “Translink comply with legislation and policy regarding seat belts on home-to-school transport.  Zero emission vehicles are used as stage carriage services and not designated as pupil only.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he has considered reducing the number of Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: I fully acknowledge that people can look at the organisation and structures of our local health service, and question whether it could or should be better. That is a view that I also share. But equally, with all the immense pressures weighing down on our health service, I would question whether embarking on a major change project and all the associated upheaval should really be a priority at this time. I, however, very much see our Health and Social Care Trusts as one, working closely together to deliver the best possible outcomes for our people.  That is very much reflected in the document recently published on our hospitals – creating a network for better outcomes.  I am very clear that our Trusts need to increasingly work collaboratively to deliver hospital services in a networked way to maximise good outcomes for our people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline how many businesses have benefited from the 50 per cent rates discount since the launch of the Back in Business scheme in May 2024.
To ask the Minister of Education whether discussions have progressed with the Education Authority and the Ulster University regarding a site for the new Causeway Academy.
answer_text: I appreciate that the long-term goal for the new Causeway Academy is to have all pupils educated in a new build solution, potentially on the Ulster University site. However, this will require major capital investment and at present there is no open call for major capital works.Should the school be announced for major capital works in the future, the preferred site will ultimately be identified through the business case process. However, consistent with the Case for Change, arrangements are being made for the Chief Executive of the Education Authority to meet with senior officials of the Ulster University for a ‘without prejudice’ discussion on how specific solutions at the Coleraine site could be developed in the longer term. These strategic discussions will concentrate on the potential for site options.The Education Authority has advised that the feasibility study for the “Day One” solution for September 2026 is well underway and is planned to be completed by the end of November 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how he will ensure that a balanced approach is taken to the development of Ulster University’s three campus model.
answer_text: I fully support and recognise the benefits of Ulster University’s three-campus model in providing accessible higher education to students from all communities.While the expansion of Ulster University’s Magee campus is a priority in the draft Programme for Government, my Department will also continue to collaborate closely with the university to support the sustainability of all their campuses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline his Department’s plans to work with Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council to maximise the tourism potential arising from The Open at Royal Portrush in 2025.
To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether any booms have been installed in Lough Neagh to prevent the spread of algae into the Lower Bann.
answer_text: My Department has not installed physical booms to prevent the migration of algae from Lough Neagh into the Lower Bann.  The blue green algae, identified in Lough Neagh in 2023 and 2024, are microscopic Cyanobacteria which, due to their specific properties and the dynamic environment in Lough Neagh, are distributed throughout the water column. The deployment of booms, which extend approx 1 metre from the surface into the water column, would not be effective in preventing the migration of the Cyanobacteria from the Lough to the Lower Bann River. The force and volume of water moving from the lough to the river would also render booms ineffective.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the additional funding provided to local councils to ensure the enforcement of legislation relating to XL Bully type dogs.
answer_text: I remain committed to providing support for costs incurred by councils as they implement and enforce XL Bully legislation.  My Department has a budget of £100k which, in addition to any claims for compensation, is available to provide additional training of dog wardens, updating IT systems and other associated enforcement needs related to the safeguarding measures introduced for XL Bully type dogs.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of fines in the last 12 months for XL Bully dog owners who have not registered their animal, broken down by local council area.
answer_text: Owners of XL Bully dogs have until 31 December 2024 to apply to their local council for an Exemption Certificate.  Beyond 1 January 2025, it will be illegal to own an XL Bully dog that has not been exempted.  Responsibility for enforcing any breaches of these rules will sit with councils.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether the recruitment campaign for the Northern Ireland Prison Service has commenced.
To ask the Minister of Education whether any consideration has been given to providing long-term substitute teachers with the same rights as other employees in terms of holiday and sick pay.
answer_text: A teacher engaged and paid for each day worked at a rate of 1/195 of the annual salary of a full-time permanent teacher receives a higher daily rate to account for holiday pay. Since 2004 the following statement has been included on the pay slips of supply teachers: ‘Holiday pay is included within your daily rate of pay calculated as annual salary over 195 working days’. Teachers’ (including supply teachers’) terms & conditions are negotiated and agreed by the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) which comprises representatives of teachers’ employers and sectoral bodies, the Department of Education and the five main teachers’ unions. Decisions are communicated to teachers and their employers via TNC Circulars.Through the TNC, and with limited resources, my officials are working with the employers of teachers to improve employment rights for supply teachers.A review of the use of supply teachers has been carried out through the TNC and the recommendations from this review are currently being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the action his Department is taking to safeguard community pharmacies.
answer_text: My Department recognises the essential role that community pharmacies have in delivering health services to communities in Northern Ireland and has repeatedly acknowledged the concerns raised by Community Pharmacy NI (CPNI), the representative body for community pharmacy owners, and by individual contractors themselves in respect of drug pricing increases and differences due to inflationary and cost of living increases generally.My predecessor, Minister Swann, increased funding to community pharmacy bringing the opening position for community pharmacy to £147m this year, an increase of £19m from 2023/24. This is the highest level of funding to date in NI, which compares favourably to the rest of the UK, and represents a 40% increase in funding over the last 6 years. This is against a background of the Department facing a significant funding gap to maintain existing services, and the wider HSC continuing to experience an extremely unpredictable and volatile financial position, which we are continuing to keep under review. With regards to the valuable role that community pharmacy has within health and social care, I am happy to report that a Strategic Plan for Community Pharmacy 2030 was launched by my Department in May 2024. It is available at:  https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/publications/community-pharmacy-strategic-plan-2030 This Plan aims to fully realise the potential of community pharmacy services to support better health outcomes from medicines and prevent illness. Implementation of the strategy will not only support elderly and vulnerable patients but the whole population. This important piece of work was developed collaboratively by my Department and CPNI, and describes the vision and strategic priorities for community pharmacy up to 2030.My Department will continue to work with CPNI, SPPG and the pharmacy profession to progress a phased approach to implementation of the Strategic Plan over the coming years and to subject to funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) what evaluation has been carried out on the No Mow May approach; and (ii) whether any evaluation has highlighted road safety concerns with high grass at road ends and junctions.
answer_text: Road safety is always our priority and we will continue to cut sight lines at junctions to ensure visibility for road-users. The No Mow May approach is not prioritised over road safety. My Department is committed to protecting wildlife and promoting biodiversity when managing roadside verges. My Department’s approach to verge management aims to maintain the right balance between road safety and the control of grass and weeds, while achieving a greater focus on environmental protection. By reducing the areas of verge cut on many roads to a single swath or, where possible without compromising on road safety, reducing to a single cut later in the season, it is possible to increase the focus on environmental benefit. The month of May is very important as bees and butterflies are actively foraging for food, sourced from dandelions and other flowering plants, found in grassy areas. Because of the extent of roadside verges to be maintained, my Department’s grass cutting season commences in April on a scheduled basis. The schedule includes sightlines across the network, and only those areas that can be left uncut without comprising road safety are left unmown. By refraining from unnecessary grass-cutting during May, where possible, we are also helping to tackle pollution as well as locking away the very significant carbon below our feet. This approach will deliver benefits in promoting biodiversity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the grants currently available to homeowners who wish to install renewable energy sources in their properties.
answer_text: There is currently no financial support available from my Department for homeowners who wish to install renewable energy sources in their homes.		The Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022 set out a target of 80% of electricity consumption to be from renewable sources in the north of Ireland by 2030. Essential to meeting this target will be the establishment of a support scheme for renewable electricity.		Departmental officials are currently working on a scheme design that will incentivise investment in larger scale renewable electricity projects, the high-level design of this scheme was published in April. Options for the installation of domestic renewable energy sources will continue to be evaluated as part of our net zero ambitions.		As you may be aware, the Windsor Framework enables the British government to extend the VAT relief for Energy Saving Materials, previously available in Britian to the north. This means that people here will also be able to apply zero rates of VAT to the installation of energy-saving materials such as solar panels.		Homeowners may also wish to seek independent advice on renewable energy sources, and energy efficiency matters, from organisations such as the NI Energy Advice Service, the Energy Saving Trust and Citizen’s Advice. These technologies have the potential to provide an attractive payback to homeowners without grant support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what discussions his Department has had with the (i) Education Authority; and (ii) Ulster University in relation to a site for the new Causeway Academy, Coleraine.
answer_text: The new Causeway Academy, Coleraine is the result of an amalgamation of North Coast Integrated College, Coleraine College and Dunluce School. As a controlled school, the Education Authority (EA) is the school managing authority and the EA is progressing a project to locate the three former schools onto a single site by September 2026, subject to available funding. The new school will operate from existing accommodation until it is approved for major capital investment.Causeway Academy has not been included in a Major Works announcement and while initial discussions have taken place with Ulster University, the preferred site will ultimately be identified through the business case process, should the school be announced for major capital investment in the future. Discussions with Ulster University will continue to be explored, consistent with the longer term vision for the new school, as described in the Case for Change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the options available to homeowners in new residential areas where developers have gone bankrupt, but street lighting and roads have not yet been adopted by his Department.
answer_text: All new developments are subject to the Private Streets (NI) Order 1980 and the Private Streets (Amendment) Order 1992, which require developers to enter into an agreement with the Department, to provide the roads, footways and sewers to the Department’s standards. This agreement is secured by a bond, that may be used by DfI Roads to complete the road works should the developer default.If you require additional information on specific developments, please contact dfiroads.western@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the timescale for a new build for the new Causeway Academy, Coleraine.
answer_text: The new Causeway Academy, Coleraine is the result of an amalgamation of North Coast Integrated College, Coleraine College and Dunluce School. It will operate from existing accommodation until such time as it is approved for major capital investment. The Education Authority has initiated a project to locate the three schools onto a single site by September 2026, subject to available funding.  Planning for a new build school will only commence should the project be included in a future Major Works Capital announcement. Given the current budget constraints and the ongoing significant pressures in regard to Special Educational Needs and urgent health and safety works, I cannot give any commitment on when there may be a future call for Major Works projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what specialist autism mental health services are available in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: It is accepted that autistic people are at higher risk of having negative mental health outcomes. While it is vital that such individuals have good and equitable access to mental health services, and that those services are able to cope with their specific needs, it is not considered appropriate to develop specialist autism mental health services designed to treat those individuals.Mental Health teams include psychiatrists, psychologists, therapists, nurses, social workers and other professionals who specialise in working with children and families. Mental Health teams can diagnose and support neurodevelopmental conditions such as autism and ADHD.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the increasing challenges that are impacting pupil attendance, what reviews are planned of the existing attendance strategy.
answer_text: My officials are currently reviewing and refreshing the existing attendance strategy in order to take account of challenges facing our children and young people to attend school regularly.It is important that the draft strategy reflects the views of children and young people, and the Education Authority’s Youth Service has agreed to undertake this work in Autumn 2024. The draft strategy will be consulted upon thereafter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has allocated funding from his Department's budget to allow the continuation of the Being Well Doing Well Programme.
answer_text: I am currently finalising the Department’s 2024-25 budget strategy.  Each of the Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework projects, including Being Well Doing Well, have been advised on interim budgets to use for planning purposes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of proposed housing developments that are unable to proceed currently due to wastewater infrastructure issues, broken down by (i) location; and (ii) unit density.
answer_text: The impact of public wastewater infrastructure issues on proposed housing developments is an operational matter for NI Water. To enable connection NI Water must make investment to upgrade pumps, replace sewer mains or increase capacity at a treatment works to accommodate these developments.
