Numbers rounded to the nearest 100.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.comMaking direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline what steps her Department will take to achieve a regional balance when placing Northern Ireland Civil Service staff in posts.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the format of the form sent to GPs in relation to a Personal Independence Payment claimant; and (ii) how long GPs are given to complete this.
answer_text: The form sent to GPs about a Personal Independent Payment claimants is known as a GP Factual Report.  The Report seeks the GPs knowledge of the claimant and their medical record, specifically, information on health conditions or disabilities impacting the claimant’s functional ability. The Report is comprised of the following sections: Disabling conditions; History of condition(s); Symptoms and variability; Relevant clinical findings; Treatment; Effects of the condition(s) on day-to-day life, and any other information considered relevant.  The Report also asks about any history of threatening or violent behaviour, and if the claimant could travel to a consultation centre by public transport or taxi.(ii) 	GPs are asked to complete and return the Report within 10 days.  If a response is not received by day 15, the case is progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Personal Independence Payment claimants that have had award decisions changed following receipt of the GP report requested by the Department in the last 12 months.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information in the format requested.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the steps his Department is taking to ensure that medical evidence is available to Capita at the time of a Personal Independence Payment assessment.
answer_text: The PIP assessment is functional rather than clinical and further medical evidence is not required in all cases. Capita, however, consider the need for further medical evidence as part of the Initial Review completed on every case and where required, will request additional information from health professionals identified by the customer. Capita currently requests further medical evidence in around 32% of cases.		In response to the Northern Ireland Public Service Ombudsman’s report on PIP, my Department has implemented a range of measures to improve awareness about the role that available medical evidence can play in determining entitlement.  These include changes to customer messaging to highlight the type of evidence which may be useful and the introduction of discussion about medical evidence as part of each new claim call.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether periodic awareness training is undertaken for midwives in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust for uncommon diseases, such as necrotising enterocolitis, in newborns.
answer_text: There are no formal periodic awareness sessions undertaken by midwives in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust for uncommon diseases in newborns such as necrotising enterocolitis.  Midwifery students are provided with this education and knowledge as part of their undergraduate training programme.  However, learning from cases and awareness of symptomatology are discussed at various professional forums which midwives access, such as the Perinatal meeting, Morbidity and Mortality meetings and Audit and Governance meetings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how adults who receive a formal diagnosis of ADHD or ASD following a private consultation can access appropriate medication to manage their symptoms.
answer_text: Regarding medication after private assessment, the position in respect of someone electing to seek diagnosis and treatment from a private provider is as follows: - If the private provider subsequently recommends a treatment, the GP will consider whether they can prescribe that treatment. This will be based upon the GP’s professional judgement whether to prescribe or not. When a specialist treatment is recommended for prescribing by a consultant within Health and Social Care (HSC) arrangements, the GP will normally require a shared care agreement in place. There are regionally specified and agreed shared arrangements that have been developed for use within the HSC. It is highly unlikely that a GP would accept to prescribe a specialist treatment at the request of a private consultant, as to do so would expose them to substantial professional risk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what checks are carried out to ensure that tanning salons are following the Sunbeds Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
answer_text: In accordance with the provisions of the Sunbeds Act (Northern Ireland) 2011, all District Councils send an advisory letter annually to all sunbed businesses, all premises are inspected once every two years and all businesses are Test Purchased once every two years.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what steps his Department is taking to ensure that medical evidence is available to Capita at the time of a Personal Independence Payment assessment.
To ask the Minister of Education (i) how many schools in East Londonderry have signed up to the Being Well Doing Well Programme; and (ii) what plans are in place to roll this out to other schools.
answer_text: Currently there are nine schools in East Londonderry who are engaged in the programme which commenced in the current academic year.  This includes seven primary schools, one special school and one post-primary school.Roll out of the programme is dependent on continued funding.  Subject to budget confirmation, it is anticipated that a further 150 schools would begin the programme in September 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the average waiting time for access to an Education Welfare Officer.
answer_text: In the 2023/24 academic year, for the cases that have been allocated to an Education Welfare Officer, the average time from being received until allocation was 24.5 days. There are currently 1,916 cases on the waiting list, with waiting times as given below:Unallocated Open Cases - Waiting Time No. of Cases0 - 2 weeks1123 - 12 weeks92713 - 36 weeks34037 - 60 weeks31160+ weeks226Total1,916

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action his Department taking to address student absence in schools.
answer_text: Increased rates of pupil absence are a significant and on-going challenge.  Whilst we must have a universal approach to supporting pupil attendance, I am mindful that there are vulnerable groups of children and young people who require additional support.  These groups can include those entitled to free school meals, pupils with special educational needs, ethnic minority children and care experienced children. My Department, the Education Authority and schools have a range of measures in place to support pupils. These include DE’s “Miss School. Miss Out Strategy”, “The Challenge of Improving Pupil Attendance at School - Case Studies paper” and the Absence Tiers Guidance. The Education and Training Inspectorate also published a series of case studies in 2016 which schools can continue to draw upon.I am conscious of the increasing challenges that are adversely affecting pupil attendance and my officials are currently reviewing and refreshing the existing attendance strategy in order to take account of these circumstances.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on the implementation of the High Street Task Force report Delivering a 21st Century High Street.
To ask the Minister of Education whether any additional funding will be provided to primary schools with nurturing units.
answer_text: Whilst Departmental Budgets for 2024-25 have not yet been confirmed, the Department has been provided with an initial planning baseline by the Department of Finance which is unchanged from the 2023-24 position. The final 2024-25 budget allocation in respect of Nurture provision will be decided upon following confirmation of the Department’s overall budget allocation.In the longer-term, officials have commenced work to develop options and associated funding recommendations for the Department’s future support of Nurture provision.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether there are any plans to carry out a review of security across all constituency offices.
answer_text: The security of Members is something the Assembly Commission takes extremely seriously and was on the agenda of recent meetings before the Assembly Commission was reconstituted.The PSNI have historically taken the lead in terms of personal security and crime prevention advice, and they have provided this on an individual basis to Members upon request. This has included carrying out survey visits to constituency offices, and the provision of advice and recommendations relative to personal and physical security measures. On 21 December 2023, the Clerk/Chief Executive wrote to the Chief Constable to highlight further concerns raised by some Members regarding their personal security and that of constituency office staff, and enquired as to what more might be done by PSNI by way of advice and guidance, and whether PSNI would be in a position to offer the provision of further security surveys at constituency offices and Members private dwellings.  The Chief Constable subsequently confirmed that PSNI would take this forward, and Personal Security Training for Members will now be provided by the PSNI in Parliament Buildings on 30 April 2024 for Members and their staff. This will include the provision of advice on personal security and lone working, and will also provide an opportunity for Members only, to directly request security surveys of their constituency offices, and homes.  The PSNI advise that a number of surveys have already been carried out at Members’ request.In August 2020, the Assembly Commission published the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) (Amendment) Determination (NI) which increased the quantum of Constituency Office Establishment Expenses from £2,000 to £4,000, specifically to provide additional funding for health and safety and security measures for Members. The Determination also specifically permitted Members to recover the cost of security measures from their £7,000 Constituency Office Operating Expenses allowance.  At its meeting on 29 November 2023, the Assembly Commission discussed initial proposals to further increase Constituency Office Establishment Expenses, for exclusive use in the provision of additional security measures and intends to consider this further at its next meetings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans are in place to enhance facilities at the Ulster University, Coleraine campus.
answer_text: I support Ulster University’s commitment to its multi-campus mission and I intend to work with all our universities to help ensure their campuses are thriving, sustainable centres for higher education.As autonomous bodies, it is a matter for universities to develop and communicate plans for improved campus facilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what plans are in place to increase student numbers at the Ulster University, Coleraine.
answer_text: I have already met with both Ulster University and Queen’s University Belfast for wide-ranging discussions which included consideration of the impact of the maximum student number cap.  I intend to work with our universities to help ensure all their campuses are thriving, sustainable centres for higher education.  However, it is for the Higher Education providers, as autonomous institutions, to determine where to locate courses across their campuses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to enhance services at the Causeway Hospital, Coleraine.
answer_text: Causeway Hospital is and will remain an important part of our acute hospital network. The Northern Health and Social Care Trust remains committed to maintaining acute inpatient services and a 24/7 Emergency Department at  the Causeway Hospital. The Trust are eager to explore options to further develop the Causeway site. I am assured that Northern Trust have been engaging with staff and other stakeholders as they develop a vision for the future of the hospital.  The Trust hope to publicly launch their vision for Causeway Hospital in the spring.Transformation is needed in how we deliver services across the health and social care system as a whole, and work is currently underway to develop a regional blueprint in relation to Northern Ireland’s hospital network. We believe there is an opportunity for Causeway Hospital to be developed to better serve the changing needs of the local population, and enhance the site, growing same day emergency care, elective and cancer services.  Causeway can be an exemplar model of how to do joined-up, integrated care, and is perfectly positioned to become an elective and diagnostic hub for the North West.   I understand Northern Trust are also taking the opportunity to reshape mental health provision to better meet the needs of their population.Causeway Hospital has a very bright future ahead, demonstrated by the continued investment in services there. In the past year alone, there has been investment of £1.8m into urgent and ambulatory care, £1.2m into solar energy, and £1m in the form of a new state-of-the-art CT scanner.  The Trust also expect to add an MRI scanner to this list of available diagnostic services. By focussing on the right services, attracting investment and building workforce, the Trust is confident that Causeway Hospital can continue to thrive and serve the local population for many years to come.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what discussions he has had with officials to reduce waiting lists.
answer_text: Tackling our lengthy waiting times is one of my priorities and is indeed an Executive priority.  It is, undoubtedly a long-term issue that requires sustainable and recurrent funding, workforce development and system wide transformation.I have had intensive discussions with my officials regarding the waiting list situation and there has been considerable progress in implementing the Elective Care Framework. Our waiting lists are unacceptable, and the need to transform elective services on a sustainable basis is paramount.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) his assessment of the current water and sewerage capacity in East Londonderry; and (ii) the impact this has on future development.
answer_text: The issues you raised are operational matters for NI Water.  You should therefore, direct your question to NI Water, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what plans he has to increase the number of Housing Executive temporary accommodation properties in the Causeway Coast and Glens area.
answer_text: The number of Housing Executive temporary accommodation properties in the Causeway Coast and Glens area is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.  You should therefore direct your question to the Housing Executive’s dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to reduce the turnaround time for blue badge questionnaires sent to GP surgeries for completion.
answer_text: Completing application forms for blue badges is non-GMS work and as such, GPs are not contractually obliged to undertake this work.  Due to increasing demand and pressure within the healthcare system, non-contractual work may be declined so that GMS services can be maintained. When applying for a blue badge, a large proportion of applicants will not require medical evidence and other forms of supporting documents such as a PIP statement of entitlement or DLA letter among others will suffice.  Where medical evidence is requested, this could be provided from hospital consultants or GPs.Advice regarding entitlement to a blue badge and the application process can be accessed via the NI Direct Government Services website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether consideration could be made for the provision of clinical psychology bereavement support for mothers following infant death, in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: In the Northern Health and Social Care Trust, neonatal services provide clinical psychological support for mothers and their families while their infant is an inpatient in the Neonatal Unit. Psychological support is also available for families where the baby is receiving end of life care. Following the death of an infant, a Consultant Clinical Psychologist can provide follow up psychological support for parents and siblings if required or will signpost the family to community bereavement services.Whilst there is no identified clinical psychology resource exclusively for maternity services, the Psychological Therapy Service has a perinatal pathway in line with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidance, which includes women in the antenatal, perinatal and postnatal period up to 12 months post-delivery. Referrals on this pathway are prioritised and the service aims to see women within three months for intervention, dependant on availability of clinicians. Mothers and families experiencing loss have the support of a specialist midwifery bereavement service.  A listening service provides support and guidance for bereaved mothers, partners and their families and is available to all parents who experience pregnancy loss at any gestation, stillbirth, early neonatal death, termination for medical reasons, and new pregnancy after loss, for up to 28 days following delivery of the baby.Further bereavement care and support, including clinical psychological support or therapeutic counselling, can be provided through a number of pathways, such as a referral to specialist midwives, the General Practitioner, a community mental health team or psychological therapies.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what support he can provide to community pharmacists to ensure they can maintain and continue to supply medicines to patients.
answer_text: I very much appreciate the extremely important work that community pharmacies deliver for our population with respect to the supply of medicines. I have received representation from pharmacy contractors and from their representative body, CPNI which highlights issues in respect of funding.An urgent meeting was held this week with representatives of CPNI to consider the funding issues. I have asked my Department to address some of the pressures that pharmacies are facing within what is available and affordable. I encouraged CPNI to continue to work with Department officials to seek ways to ensure the pharmacy service can develop in line with our shared strategic objectives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to extend the eligibility criteria of the SureStart programme.
answer_text: The Sure Start programme is delivered via 38 Sure Start projects, in (at least) the 25% most disadvantaged areas in Northern Ireland as determined by postcode.  All children aged 0-3 years (and their families) within the catchment areas of the 38 projects are eligible to access services.  Around 41,500 children aged 0-3 years, and families, can avail of Sure Start support.Expansion of early years interventions currently funded by my Department (including Sure Start) is being considered within the development of an Executive Early Learning and Childcare (ELC) strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what her Department is doing to address the findings of the latest Criminal Justice Inspection report on HMP Magilligan.
answer_text: I am pleased that inspectors recognised where progress had been made in their recent Independent Review of Progress at Magilligan Prison; however, I appreciate that there are areas where more work is required.The Governor of Magilligan Prison, in conjunction with health and education partners, is implementing a plan to address the inspection recommendations and to build upon the improvements identified by inspectors.  Progress made since the inspection was completed includes embedding a drug strategy, completing a review of the education curriculum, and implementing a “Step up” programme to support progression from entry-level education courses.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department has considered any new technology for fixing potholes.
answer_text: My Department works closely with the construction industry to monitor new technology and innovations in respect of pothole repairs.The Department keeps informed about new approaches to road maintenance through its engagement with other road authorities, Professional Engineering Institutions, universities and the wider construction and infrastructure industry. We are very open to adopting new approaches and innovative materials that offer an improvement to our current approach to road defect repair, and are actively exploring how emerging technology can be used to better maintain the road network.     My Department will continue to liaise with contractors and suppliers to identify any opportunities in the future that could improve the repair of road defects.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when funding allocated for the Sub Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer will be released.
answer_text: I recognise and share the deep frustration of many that this programme has not been delivered. Work is progressing to develop proposals for the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Football and I have asked my officials to present me with proposals and options for consideration as a matter of priority. Once final proposals are confirmed and approvals are secured, I will be able to provide a timeframe for the process to apply for funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many pothole repairs are currently outstanding in the East Londonderry constituency.
answer_text: Following the reform of local government in 2014, the Department collects and stores data in a manner which mirrors the boundaries of the new district council areas. Road surface defects, including potholes, are managed through the Roads Maintenance Client Management System which records the total number of defects and the number outstanding for each local maintenance office area.The data is regularly published through the OpenDataNI portal at https://www.opendatani.gov.uk/dataset/surface-defects.Official Statistics on the condition of our roads are also now published through the Northern Ireland Road Network and Condition Statistics.  The 2022-23 publication (the first such publication) can be found at https://datavis.nisra.gov.uk/infrastructure/northern-ireland-road-network-and-condition-statistics-2223.html.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many dental practices in East Londonderry are still taking on NHS patients.
answer_text: Unfortunately due to there being no Minister in post after midnight on the 27th of October, my Department will be unable to answer the question.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what discussions his Department has had with (i) Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council; and (ii) Invest NI to encourage new tenants for the Enterprise Zone in Coleraine.
answer_text: Lead responsibility for development and marketing of the Atlantic Link Enterprise Campus (ALEC) sits with the Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Council (CC&GBC), as landowners. My Department’s responsibility is to ensure that the ALEC is developed by CC&GBC in a way which will attract capital intensive companies.  I met with representatives from CC&GBC on 2 September 2021, to discuss ways in which my Department could support the actions they had planned to attract new tenants.  A Monitoring and Evaluation Framework between my Department and the Council is in place which sets out key milestones proposed by CC&GBC for the development of the ALEC, including information events and marketing, and provides updates on progress. The latest monitoring report, to end September 2022, was received from CC&GBC in October 2022. It is currently under consideration by my officials.Invest NI has an ongoing role to assist with promotional events to potential investors at the British Embassy in Dublin and the Guildhall in London. The International Investment Division are fully aware of the potential benefits of the ALEC. The entire International Team (consisting of c.100 staff) have visited the Campus and continue to facilitate introductions to relevant events, conferences, and contacts in GB, RoI, and the US. Invest NI also promote the Campus on the property section of its website.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) whether households who have coal as the only means of heating their homes will be entitled to assistance under any of the cost of living energy schemes; and (ii) how will this payment be made to them.
answer_text: On 21st September the UK Government (UKG) outlined new plans to help cut energy bills and gave further details on the support available for households in Great Britain and Northern Ireland. 		As part of this announcement, households who are connected to the electricity network but who use alternative fuels other than gas to heat their homes will still receive support through the Energy Price Guarantee for their electricity costs, as well as the £400 through the Energy Bills Support Scheme. The government also announced that it will provide an additional payment of £100 to NI consumers to compensate for price increases in those alternative fuels with the final details of how the payment will be delivered still to be agreed. I have called on UKG to reconsider this level of support for alternative fuel consumers. 	It is vital that all of these support measures are rolled out as quickly as possible so that local business and domestic consumers are supported as their energy consumption rises into the winter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how often PSNI officers have been redeployed to cover G4S security and clerical staff in police stations, in the last six months.
answer_text: The deployment of PSNI officers, including to cover G4S security and clerical staff, is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. Therefore, you may wish to contact the PSNI directly for this information.  I am committed to respecting the independence of both organisations.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, following the initial Education Authority consultation, what are the next steps in the proposal to merge Coleraine College, Dunluce School and North Coast Integrated College.
answer_text: On 9 March 2022, the Education Authority (EA) published a series of Development Proposals (DPs) in relation to the Causeway Area.  DPs 676–680 propose the closure of Coleraine College, Dunluce School and North Coast Integrated College (IC) and the establishment of a new Controlled IC which will include Autism specific and Learning Support Classes.  The statutory two-month objection period concluded on 23 May 2022.My officials are currently collating all responses received by the Department, along with all other pertinent information and will provide me with a detailed submission to assist me in taking decisions that is in the best interest of all learners.Unfortunately, I am unable to offer a specific date for a decision.  I appreciate the importance of a timely decision regarding the proposals and I am acutely aware of the need for certainty for the interested parties.  I will not delay a decision any longer than necessary.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what measures are in place to ensure that utility companies return surfaces to an acceptable standard after work is carried out.
answer_text: Utility companies are responsible for the permanent reinstatement of roads to an agreed specification. Where a utility company does not reinstate a road in a satisfactory manner, the Department applies its procedures for inspection of reinstatements and dealing with reinstatement failures as set out in the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) Code of Practice for Inspections.  When a utility company fails to carry out the reinstatement to an acceptable standard, the utility company responsible for the repair or replacement is duly notified. Where necessary, additional inspections are then carried out by my officials, at the utility company’s expense, to confirm that any defects identified have been corrected.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) who is responsible for enforcing the law in relation to cycling on pavements; and (ii) how many prosecutions have been made in the last three years.
answer_text: The Highway Code is the official guide to using our roads safely and legally and it is the responsibility for every road user to be aware of its contents for the safety of themselves and other road users.  Many of the rules of the Highway Code are legal requirements and if you disobey these rules you are committing a criminal offence.  Such rules will contain the words MUST/MUST NOT and also state the effective legislation.  Rules 59-85 in the Highway Code pertain to cyclists, In particular rule 64 states ”You MUST NOT cycle on a footway or footpath unless on a cycle track where one has been provided”.  The Road Traffic Regulation (NI) order 1997 refers.As enforcement is the responsibility of the PSNI, the Department does not hold any records on the number of prosecutions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance how many NICS staff are currently making use of the Connect2 hubs in (i) Ballykelly; and (ii) Ballymena.
answer_text: The Ballykelly hub was used 30 times and the Ballymena hub was used 80 times between the 1st and the 30th September 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what options are available to NICS staff who do not wish to return to the workplace under the Hybrid Working Policy due to medical reasons.
answer_text: The NICS has recently introduced a Hybrid Working Policy which allows staff with suitable roles to have an agreed working arrangement that allows for a mix of workplace, home and remote working. It is expected that staff should attend the office in line with business need. This represents a balanced approach to work which will allow the Civil Service to offer many staff greater flexibility and lower the carbon emissions associated with reduced commuting while maintaining the important benefits associated with workplace attendance such as social interaction and opportunities for learning and mentoring from colleagues.  Staff who feel that they cannot return to the workplace due to medical reasons are advised to speak to their line manager about their individual circumstances and requirements.  Each case will be considered sensitively, taking into account the individual circumstances and relevant HR Policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there will be financial assistance under the cost of living energy payments for those who only have coal fires in their domestic properties.
answer_text: On 21st September the UK Government (UKG) outlined new plans to help cut energy bills and gave further details on the support available for households in Great Britain and Northern Ireland. 	As part of this announcement, households who are connected to the electricity network but who use alternative fuels other than gas to heat their homes will still receive support through the Energy Price Guarantee for their electricity costs, as well as the Energy Bills Support Scheme. The government also announced that it will provide an additional payment of £100 to NI consumers to compensate for price increases in those alternative fuels. I have called on UKG to reconsider this level of support for alternative fuel consumers. 		It is vital that these support measures are rolled out as quickly as possible so that local business and domestic consumers are supported as their energy consumption rises into the winter.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) why there are delays in 50 year olds getting their first breast screening; and (ii) what action is being taken to reduce the delays.
answer_text: There are no known delays in 50 years olds getting their first breast screening.  Women aged 50 to 70 who are registered with a GP, are invited every three years to the breast screening programme.  As women are invited by GP practice every three years, this means that not every woman receives her first invitation as soon as she is 50. Around a third of women will be invited for the first time before their 51st birthday (the year they turn 50), a third before their 52nd birthday (the year they turn 51) and the rest before their 53rd birthday (the year they turn 52). All eligible women should be invited for the first time before their 53rd birthday.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what her Department is doing to secure more private operators to fulfil home to school transport contracts.
answer_text: The procurement of transport providers is the responsibility of the Education Authority (EA) and it provides transport assistance for over 90,000 pupils each day.The EA has advised me it has already taken significant steps to maximise the number of private operators that deliver a service for the EA.  In 2019 it introduced a new procurement procedure, Dynamic Purchasing Systems (DPS) designed specifically to stimulate greater levels of competition for home to school bus and taxis services. The flexibility and ease of this procurement system means private operators are able to join it at any stage enabling them to obtain regular home to school transport work.  Procuring taxi services to support home to school transport has become challenging due to pressures facing the taxi industry as a whole in Northern Ireland.  The EA continues to look at innovative ways of recruiting private operators and aims to hold more engagement events with the objective of attracting interest from private operators not already doing business with the EA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what updates he has received on the rollout of the £400 Energy Bill Support Scheme in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The British Government has confirmed that it will deliver the £400 Energy Bill Support Scheme here in the North.I made it clear to the Chancellor and Secretary of State for Business, Energy and Industrial Strategy that any delay in issuing these payments to citizens here is completely unacceptable. Despite this, the British Government has yet to provide an update how or when people here will receive the payment.I have therefore written to the Chief Secretary to Treasury seeking clarification on both how and when households will receive not only the £400 payment, but also the additional energy support measures announced on 8 September.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether discretion will be shown for the late return of benefit appeals and benefit review forms in light of the ongoing postal strikes.
answer_text: My Department has already instigated processes for staff to apply flexibility and discretion to return dates for all benefit forms, where delays have been experienced due to the ongoing postal strikes. As for the time limits within which an appeal may be brought, these are set down in The Social Security and Child Support (Decisions and Appeals) Regulations 1999.Regulation 31 (6) (c) states that the time for bringing an appeal may be extended by a legally qualified member where normal postal services were disrupted. An application for an extension of time under this regulation shall be made in accordance with regulation 33 and shall be determined by a legally qualified panel member.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the road resurfacing works scheduled to take place in the East Londonderry constituency in the 2022-23 financial year.
answer_text: The Department does not hold or collate information on a constituency basis but rather it is held on a Council or DfI Roads Section Office area basis.  The East Derry constituency falls within the Causeway Coast and Glens Council Area and the local DfI Section Office is Causeway Coast and Glens West.The resurfacing programme for this Council area is, as is the case for other areas, currently being finalised following confirmation of the Department’s 2022-23 capital contingency funding allocation. Details of works programmes, including resurfacing schemes, for the 2022-23 financial year will be included in the Department’s upcoming Report to Causeway Coast and Glens Council, which will in due course be presented to Council, published on the Department’s website and placed in the Assembly library. Should you have any further questions on this matter, please contact DfI Roads Northern Division at: dfiroads.northern@infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) the criteria used by the Health and Social Care Business Support Organisation to de-register patients from a GP practice; and ii) how many patients within the East Londonderry constituency have been de-registered in the last three months.
answer_text: (i) In line with the requirements for patient deductions set out in The Health and Personal Social Services (General Medical Services Contracts) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2004 the reasons for deductions may include:Removal from the list at the request of the patientRemoval from the list at the request of the contractorRemovals from the list of patients who are violentRemovals from the list of patients registered elsewhereRemovals from the list of patients who have moved Removals from the list of patients absent from the United Kingdom etc.Removals from the list of patients accepted elsewhere as temporary residents (ii) The number of patient deductions for this constituency cannot be accurately calculated as it is not possible to fully ascertain which surgeries and patients are located within the constituency area.A figure for the deductions from the medical register based on postcodes in the environs of the specific constituency (i.e. BT46, BT47, BT49, BT51, BT52, BT53, BT55, BT56) has been calculated. Based on the information from GP surgeries within the above postcodes the number of deductions was 657.This was for the 3 months period 1st June 2022 to 31st August 2022.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what measures are currently in place to prevent further delays to the delivery of the MS drug Aubagio to patients in NI.
answer_text: I can confirm that there is no shortage of Aubagio in Northern Ireland. There have been some issues reported relating to the capacity of the main homecare company who supply and deliver this treatment to patients on behalf of the Belfast Trust. An alternative homecare provider for new patients commencing on Aubagio has been in place since April 2022.Should any patient who remains on Aubagio through the original homecare company have a concern about potentially missing a dose of treatment, they should contact their clinical team in order that a supply can be made available urgently from the Trust directly.The BSO procurement and logistics service has issued a prior information notice (PIN) to welcome expressions of interest to address any gaps in the capacity of the current pharma homecare service provision with a view to increasing the supply base within Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline (i) what discussions he has held with Firmus Gas in respect to the tariff increase announced on 06/09/2022; and (ii) what plans does his Department have to address the rising gas prices.
answer_text: The Utility Regulator, along with officials from my Department and the Consumer Council met with firmus energy recently to discuss the increase in tariff. This is part of the Utility Regulator’s regulatory processes to ensure that what consumers pay reflects the cost of providing gas. These increases are once again largely due to increases in the forecast cost of future wholesale gas.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what financial assistance is available for people who wish to become taxi drivers.
answer_text: I can advise that my Department offers no financial assistance for people who wish to become taxi drivers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what help is available for childminders in meeting higher energy bills during the cost of living crisis.
answer_text: Registered childminders are self-employed.  Their pay, or the fees charged for their service, is a contractual issue between the individual registered childminder and the parents using their service and does not fall within the remit of my Department.The implication of the cost of living crisis on individuals and businesses is recognised across Departments.  Any response to the crisis needs to be in the context of national measures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how many face to face appointments have been GP led, as opposed to nurse-led, in the past six months in GP surgeries in the Causeway area.
answer_text: The information requested is not held by the Department.GPs are independent contractors who provide primary medical services to their patients under the terms of the General Medical Services Contracts Regulations.  GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care and management of individual patients. In terms of practice arrangements, decisions about the day to day running of the surgery, including management of appointments and which healthcare professional to direct a patient to, are determined locally by the GP practice.  The structure of General Practice has been changing over the last number of years with multi-professional teams and a change in the roles of all those working within a GP practice.   The GP is therefore one of many members of a team who will be able to provide treatment, care and support to a patient.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy what help is available to homeowners who wish to install solar panels on their homes.
answer_text: There is currently no assistance available from my Department for homeowners who wish to install solar panels.The Energy Strategy for Northern Ireland (December 2021), sets the policy direction for increasing renewable energy generation and achieving a target of at least 80% of electricity consumed in Northern Ireland coming from a diverse range of renewable sources by 2030 (target established by the Climate Change Act (Northern Ireland) 2022). 		Essential to meeting this target will be establishing a support scheme(s) for renewable electricity and, under the Energy Strategy Action Plan for 2022, officials are currently focused on securing a scheme that will support large scale development offering maximum benefit to Northern Ireland as a whole while achieving the greatest value for money.  	However, in line with the objectives of the Energy Strategy to put the consumer at the heart of the energy transition and deliver a smart, decentralised energy system, we will also look at how to support other forms of generation, including micro-generating technologies such as residential solar photovoltaics, in a considered way, as we bring forward future Action Plans.		In the interim, homeowners may wish to seek independent advice on solar PV and other energy matters from organisations such as the Energy Saving Trust, Centre for Sustainable Energy or Citizen’s Advice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what plans are in place to install new charging stations for electric vehicles in the East Londonderry area.
answer_text: The current electric vehicle public charge point network is owned by the Electricity Supply Board (ESB) and is operated on a commercial basis. ESB announced in November 2021 that it had been awarded £3.27 million from the Levelling Up Fund to expand and enhance the EV charging network in the North. ESB can be contacted directly, regarding what plans are in place to install new charging stations for electric vehicles in the East Londonderry area, at ecars@esb.ie or enquiries can be sent directly to ESB, Two Gateway, East Wall Road, DUBLIN 3, D03 A995. The market is also open to other commercial operators who would wish to provide charging infrastructure. I am pleased that my Department has been able to support the FASTER Project, funded through the INTERREG VA Territorial Co-operation Programme which is managed by the Special EU Programmes Body (SEUPB). The project has received funding to install a total of 73 EV Rapid charging points across the island of Ireland and the West of Scotland by 31 May 2023. My Department will be contributing €513,945 (£455,371) of Match Funding towards the FASTER project. The responsibility for the implementation of the project rests with SEUPB and it has agreed a delivery schedule with the project partners, led by the East Border Region Group Limited (EBR). It is expected that 20-30 of these will be located across the North and EBR is best placed to advise on charge point locations.Funding opportunities for the installation of charge point infrastructure in GB-NI are provided by the Office for Zero Emission Vehicles (OZEV). My Department has made match-funding available of circa £350k, subject to public expenditure processes, from the Department’s Blue Green Fund to assist local councils making an application to the On-street Residential Charge Point Scheme (ORCS). A local council consortium, led by Derry City and Strabane District Council, submitted a joint application to the Energy Saving Trust which manages the fund on behalf of OZEV on 30 March 2022. You may wish to contact your local council to enquire about its application.My Department has also set up an EV Infrastructure Task-Force which brings together representatives from government, consumers, energy providers, industry and EV drivers to consider our EV Infrastructure requirements and to help set out a clear action plan by autumn 2022 to deliver a fit for purpose, modern EV charging network.
